home.headline: What if you could transform employee motivation for a one-time payment of $5,000?
home.subhead: Complete source code. Your infrastructure. No subscriptions, no seat fees, no renewals. The only risk is continuing to manage performance the way you always have.
home.extrinsic.heading: Telling people what to do only gets you so far
home.extrinsic.body: Set targets. Assign KPIs. Tie compensation to outcomes. This is extrinsic motivation - and it has a hard ceiling. Your people will work just hard enough to hit the number and avoid consequences. They won't internalize the goals. They won't innovate around them. They won't own them. Fifty years of research confirms this: extrinsic systems produce compliance, not commitment.
home.extrinsic.stat1.label: of managers frustrated with their review system
home.extrinsic.stat2.label: of employees who quit cite lack of appreciation - not pay
home.extrinsic.stat3.label: of employees worldwide are engaged at work
home.extrinsic.stat4.label: lost globally to disengagement every year
home.extrinsic.stat4.source: Gallup State of the Global Workplace
home.intrinsic.heading: Intrinsic motivation doesn't mean letting people do whatever they want
home.intrinsic.body: It means enforcing your organizational goals AND having your people define their own goals within that structure. Every goal in Primer serves one of three functions:
home.intrinsic.leading.heading: Predict
home.intrinsic.leading.body: Leading indicators that forecast future performance. Your teams define the metrics they believe will drive outcomes - giving them ownership over the 'how' while you retain control of the 'what.'
home.intrinsic.leading.label: Leading indicators
home.intrinsic.lagging.heading: Report
home.intrinsic.lagging.body: Lagging indicators that demonstrate results. Revenue, retention, delivery timelines - the metrics executives and board members need to see. These aren't optional. They're structural.
home.intrinsic.lagging.label: Lagging indicators
home.intrinsic.compliance.heading: Comply
home.intrinsic.compliance.body: Regulatory, legal, and board mandates that must be met. Safety certifications, audit requirements, executive directives - non-negotiable goals that exist regardless of individual motivation.
home.intrinsic.compliance.label: Mandated requirements
home.intrinsic.bridge: The difference: your people chose how to define success within these constraints. That's what transforms compliance into commitment.
home.card.stat.imposed: Intrinsic motivation explains 45% of the variance in work behaviors
home.card.stat.stale: Only 21% of employees worldwide are engaged at work
home.card.stat.priority: Employees in goal-setting are 2× as likely to have clear expectations
home.card.stat.silos: High-engagement orgs see 41% less absenteeism and 21% higher profitability
home.card.stat.thresholds: Progress in meaningful work is the #1 driver of engagement
home.card.stat.weighting: Employees with autonomy report 50% more engagement
home.card.stat.inquiry: People don't buy what you do - they buy why you do it
home.voices.heading: The people who proved it
home.voices.quote1.text: Control leads to compliance; autonomy leads to engagement.
home.voices.quote1.source: Drive: The Surprising Truth About What Motivates Us
home.voices.quote2.text: Of all the things that can boost emotions, motivation, and perceptions during a workday, the single most important is making progress in meaningful work.
home.voices.quote2.source: The Progress Principle, Harvard Business School
home.voices.quote3.text: When people are acknowledged for their work, they are willing to work harder for less pay. When they are not, motivation collapses - even when the pay stays the same.
home.motivation.heading: Fifty years of research agree: intrinsic motivation outperforms extrinsic systems
home.motivation.body: Self-Determination Theory is one of the most replicated findings in organizational psychology. When people feel autonomy over how they work, see a clear path to mastery, and connect their effort to purpose - they perform better, stay longer, and engage more deeply. Primer operationalizes these principles.
home.motivation.autonomy.heading: Autonomy
home.motivation.autonomy.body: Employees define their own thresholds and weights. They own the interpretation of what each tier means for their function. Research shows employees with autonomy over their work report 50% more engagement.
home.motivation.autonomy.source: Self-Determination Theory - Deci & Ryan, University of Rochester
home.motivation.mastery.heading: Mastery
home.motivation.mastery.body: The five-tier scale - Alarm through Optimized - creates a visible progression. Employees can see exactly where they stand and what the next level looks like. Progress in meaningful work is the single strongest driver of engagement.
home.motivation.mastery.source: The Progress Principle - Teresa Amabile, Harvard Business School
home.motivation.purpose.heading: Purpose
home.motivation.purpose.body: Weighted metrics force explicit prioritization. When a leader assigns 40% to safety and 25% to budget, they've made a strategic statement about what matters most. Purpose isn't a poster on the wall - it's a weight in the system.
home.motivation.research.heading: What the research shows
home.motivation.research.stat1: Intrinsic motivation explains 45% of the variance in work behaviors and well-being
home.motivation.research.stat1.source: Meta-analysis of 124 studies on Self-Determination Theory
home.motivation.research.stat2: Only 21% of employees worldwide are engaged at work - costing $438 billion in lost productivity
home.motivation.research.stat2.source: Gallup State of the Global Workplace, 2025
home.motivation.research.stat3: Organizations with high engagement see 21% higher profitability and 41% less absenteeism
home.motivation.research.stat4: Employees involved in goal-setting are 2x as likely to have clear expectations and 12% more productive
home.motivation.talks.heading: Hear it from them
home.motivation.talks.dan_pink.title: The Puzzle of Motivation
home.motivation.talks.dan_pink.speaker: Daniel Pink
home.motivation.talks.dan_pink.descripti: There is a mismatch between what science knows and what business does. The secret to high performance isn't rewards and punishments, but the drive toward autonomy, mastery, and purpose.
home.motivation.talks.dan_ariely.title: What Makes Us Feel Good About Our Work?
home.motivation.talks.dan_ariely.speaker: Dan Ariely
home.motivation.talks.dan_ariely.descrip: When our work is acknowledged and we see progress, we are willing to work harder. When it is ignored, motivation collapses - even when the pay stays the same.
home.motivation.talks.barry_schwartz.tit: The Way We Think About Work Is Broken
home.motivation.talks.barry_schwartz.des: We design workplaces that strip out meaning, then wonder why people are disengaged. It's time to stop treating workers as cogs on a wheel.
home.motivation.talks.angela_duckworth.t: Grit: The Power of Passion and Perseverance
home.motivation.talks.angela_duckworth.d: The best predictor of success isn't IQ or talent - it's grit. Sustained effort toward long-term goals matters more than any single measurement.
home.motivation.talks.csikszentmihalyi.d: When challenge matches skill, people enter a state of flow - complete immersion in meaningful work. The five-tier framework creates this clarity.
home.motivation.talks.simon_sinek.title: How Great Leaders Inspire Action
home.motivation.talks.simon_sinek.descri: People don't buy what you do, they buy why you do it. Great leaders start with purpose - and the best frameworks make that purpose structural, not aspirational.
home.motivation.reading.heading: Further reading
home.motivation.reading.progress: The Power of Small Wins - Harvard Business Review
home.motivation.reading.gallup: Gallup State of the Global Workplace 2025
home.motivation.reading.aristotle: Google's Project Aristotle - What Makes Teams Effective
home.motivation.connection: Primer doesn't just measure performance. It gives leaders autonomy over their definitions, a mastery scale they can see, and purpose they helped create. That's the difference between compliance and ownership.
home.platform.heading: Secure goal management that builds intrinsic motivation into your organizational structure
home.platform.body: Primer gives your teams the structure to understand your requirements and demonstrate autonomy, mastery, and purpose - the three proven drivers of intrinsic motivation. At the same time, colleagues at the same level can see what each other is working toward - not how they're scoring against it - so priorities stay aligned without turning peer visibility into collective performance assessment.
home.platform.autonomy.heading: Autonomy through ownership
home.platform.autonomy.body: Employees define their own thresholds for every metric - what Alarm, Concern, Content, Effective, and Optimized look like in their function. They assign weights to reflect their strategic priorities. The result: goals they own, not goals imposed on them.
home.platform.mastery.heading: Mastery through visibility
home.platform.mastery.body: The five-tier scale creates a clear progression path. Employees can see exactly where they stand, what the next level looks like, and how their weighted composite score evolves over time. Progress becomes visible and meaningful.
home.platform.purpose.heading: Purpose through alignment
home.platform.purpose.body: Colleagues at the same level see each other's goals and priorities - what matters to each function and how it's weighted. They don't see each other's scores or tier placement. The aim is alignment across functions, not collective performance assessment.
home.platform.inquiry.heading: Structured inquiry keeps it honest
home.platform.inquiry.body: Employees can challenge their own metrics or a peer's metric through a formal inquiry process. No backchannels, no politics - transparent accountability that keeps goals current and aligned.
home.credibility.heading: One payment. Own it forever.
home.credibility.body: Complete source code. Deploy on your own infrastructure. No subscriptions, seat fees, or renewals. David PM never sees, hosts, or has access to your data.
home.close.heading: Transform motivation for $5,000 - or keep paying the cost of disengagement
home.close.body: Every cycle your leadership team runs on imposed targets and extrinsic pressure is a cycle of compliance theater. Primer replaces it with a system your leaders actually own - one that predicts, reports, and complies while building the intrinsic motivation that drives real performance.

pricing.heading: Why $5,000
pricing.intro: The price reflects what the product is: a genuinely developed system that provides value for a reasonable price.
pricing.gimmick.heading: It's not a gimmick.
pricing.gimmick.body: We're not underpricing to capture market share and raise prices later. We're not using freemium to harvest data. We're not creating artificial tiers to push you toward enterprise pricing. $5,000 is the price.
pricing.trap.heading: It's not a trap.
pricing.trap.body: There are no per-seat fees. No usage-based charges. No annual renewals. No maintenance contracts. The $5,000 is the complete cost. You own the result. Forever.
pricing.economics.heading: It reflects the economics.
pricing.economics.body: You could build this yourself. In a world of AI-assisted development, sophisticated software is more accessible than ever. But you would spend more than $5,000 in developer time just getting to a working system. Primer is already a working system. Deploy it today, use it today. The $5,000 buys you the finished product, not a half-built one you still have to complete.
pricing.constraints.heading: It reflects your constraints.
pricing.constraints.body: You haven't built anything similar because you were busy operating your business. Primer works out of the box. Deploy it today and start using it today. Customization is optional, not required. If you ever want to change the terminology, adjust the colors, or add integrations to your existing systems, you own the source code and can do it on your schedule. But nothing about the product depends on you doing any of that first.
pricing.perpetual.heading: Why Perpetual
pricing.perpetual.p1: SaaS is a liability. You pay monthly. The moment you stop paying, you lose access. Every dollar spent on SaaS is gone the day the subscription ends. You own nothing.
pricing.perpetual.p2: A perpetual source code license is an asset. You pay once. The code is yours. You can use it for a year or a decade. You can modify it. You can build on it. The value compounds instead of draining.
pricing.perpetual.p3: This is the future of business software in an AI-enabled development landscape. Companies no longer need to rent access to tools they could own. What they need is a well-built foundation they can adapt. Primer provides that foundation.
pricing.investor.heading: Why No Investor Model
pricing.investor.p1: DavidPM is not trying to appeal to investors or build a billion-dollar brand based on customer lock-in.
pricing.investor.p2: The VC-backed SaaS model depends on making customers dependent. High switching costs. Proprietary data formats. Integrations designed to deepen lock-in. The product is free or cheap until you're committed, then pricing increases because you can't leave.
pricing.investor.p3: We're not interested in that game. We're building useful tools that allow companies to pursue their own vision. The relationship ends at the point of purchase. What you do with the product is up to you. We don't want ongoing control over your operations. We don't want access to your data. We want you to succeed on your own terms.
license.can.heading: What You Can Do
license.can.ready.heading: Use it out of the box.
license.can.ready.body: Primer is a complete, finished product. Install it and start using it today. Every screen, every calculation, every workflow is ready as shipped. No mandatory configuration marathon, no "phase 1" of setup before it's useful. Modification is a right you hold, not a requirement to get value.
license.can.deploy.heading: Deploy it.
license.can.deploy.body: Run the software on any infrastructure you control. On-premises servers. Cloud VMs. Containers. Your laptop for testing. No restrictions on where or how you deploy.
license.can.modify.heading: Modify it.
license.can.modify.body: Change the code however you want. Add features. Remove features. Integrate with your existing systems. The source code is yours to adapt.
license.can.rebrand.heading: Rebrand it completely.
license.can.rebrand.body: Primer ships fully branded and ready to use, but if you ever want to rebrand it, every piece is yours to change. Call it whatever you want (the product name "Primer" is a configurable string). Change the tier labels from "Alarm, Concern, Content, Effective, Optimized" to vocabulary that fits your culture. Replace the colors, fonts, and logo. None of this is required; all of it is possible.
license.can.internal.heading: Use it internally.
license.can.internal.body: Deploy it for your organization's use without restriction. Every employee, every contractor, every leadership team member.
license.can.forever.heading: Keep it forever.
license.can.forever.body: The license is perpetual. There is no expiration. You can use the software for as long as it remains useful to you.
license.cannot.heading: What You Cannot Do
license.cannot.resell.heading: Resell it.
license.cannot.resell.body: You cannot sell the source code or licenses to other organizations. The license is for your organization's use, not for redistribution.
license.cannot.whitelabel.heading: White-label it for clients.
license.cannot.whitelabel.body: If you're a consultancy, you cannot deploy instances of the software for your clients and charge them for access. Each organization needs their own license.
license.cannot.attribution.heading: Remove attribution.
license.cannot.attribution.body: The source code contains attribution to DavidPM, LLC. You can rebrand the user-facing application, but the attribution in the source code must remain.
license.updates.heading: What Happens to Updates
license.updates.p1: Yes, you can see our updates. No, they probably won't matter. This is not a SaaS. You are not submitting feature requests and waiting for the next release. Primer is a finished product on the day you deploy it. Most organizations run it as-is. Some choose to evolve their copy over time: moving buttons, adjusting colors, wiring in their own integrations. Either path is fine. Either way, by the time we ship an update, you're already running the version that fits your team.
license.updates.p2: If you want to see what we've changed, license holders can download new versions from the authenticated dashboard at any time, included in the original price. But you don't have to wait for us, and you don't have to merge anything. The point of perpetual ownership is that you stop being a customer asking permission and start being the maintainer of your own product.
license.disappear.heading: What Happens If DavidPM Disappears
license.disappear.p1: Your deployment continues to work. You own the source code. Your system has no dependency on DavidPM infrastructure. If DavidPM ceases operations tomorrow, your deployment is unaffected.
license.disappear.p2: You will not receive updates or be able to download new source code versions, but everything you have deployed will continue to function. This is the resilience that perpetual ownership provides.
security.heading: DavidPM Is Not In Your Security Loop
security.intro.p1: When you purchase a Primer license, you receive source code. You deploy it on your infrastructure. You control the database, the authentication, the network, the access controls.
security.intro.tagline: DavidPM is not in your security loop because there is no loop to be in.
security.no_data.heading: No data leaves your environment.
security.no_data.body: Your leadership assessments, your strategic priorities, your operational health data-none of it touches DavidPM systems. Ever. We don't host it. We don't process it. We don't have access to it.
security.no_callhome.heading: No call-home.
security.no_callhome.body: The application does not phone home. There are no analytics beacons, no telemetry endpoints, no license validation pings. The code runs entirely within your environment with no external dependencies on DavidPM infrastructure.
security.no_creds.heading: No vendor credentials.
security.no_creds.body: There are no DavidPM admin accounts in your system. No support backdoors. No API keys that grant us access. We cannot log in to your deployment because no mechanism exists for us to do so.
security.readable.heading: You can read every line.
security.readable.body: This is source code, not compiled binaries. Your security team can audit every function, every database query, every authentication flow. Nothing is hidden.
security.modifiable.heading: You can modify everything.
security.modifiable.body: Don't like how we handle sessions? Change it. Want to integrate with your existing identity provider? Do it. Need to add encryption at rest? Add it. The code is yours.
security.statement1: DavidPM has no database containing your information.
security.statement2: DavidPM has no API endpoint that receives data from your deployment.
security.statement3: A breach of DavidPM's systems would expose nothing about your organization.
security.compliance.heading: What This Means for Compliance
security.compliance.regulated.heading: For regulated industries:
security.compliance.regulated.body: The perpetual source code model eliminates the third-party data processor relationship. There is no BAA to negotiate, no data processing agreement to manage, no vendor security questionnaire to complete-because we never touch your data.
security.compliance.enterprise.heading: For enterprise security reviews:
security.compliance.enterprise.body: The risk profile is identical to internally developed software. Your team controls the deployment, the infrastructure, and the data. DavidPM's involvement ends at the point of purchase.
security.compliance.residency.heading: For data residency requirements:
security.compliance.residency.body: Your data lives where you put it. You choose the jurisdiction. You choose the hosting provider. You choose the database location. We have no say in the matter because we have no involvement in the matter.
security.compliance.gov.heading: For government contractors:
security.compliance.gov.body: Primer does not require FedRAMP authorization because there is no cloud service to authorize. The source code runs inside your infrastructure. If your environment is already authorized, Primer runs inside it without adding a vendor dependency.
security.tradeoff.heading: The Tradeoff
security.tradeoff.p1: You are responsible for your own security. You maintain the system. You apply updates. You configure access controls. You monitor for threats.
security.tradeoff.p2: This is not a limitation. This is the point. The alternative-ongoing vendor access for support and maintenance-reintroduces the exposure the perpetual model eliminates.
security.tradeoff.p3: If you have the technical capacity to deploy and maintain web applications, you have the capacity to secure a Primer deployment. The included documentation covers setup, configuration, and ongoing maintenance.
security.closing.p1: We do not have a SOC 2 report. We do not need one. SOC 2 audits the controls around data a vendor holds. We hold none of yours.
security.closing.p2: What we do have is source code you can read, a deployment model you control, and an architecture that makes the question of vendor data security irrelevant by design.

demo.section.score.heading: Individual scores combine into one number. Here's how.

faq.heading: Frequently Asked Questions
faq.subheading: Everything you need to know about the Primer framework, licensing, deployment, and support.
faq.what_is_grasp.a: Primer is a management framework that replaces subjective performance reviews with objective, tiered metric evaluation. Employees participate in defining metrics, setting tier thresholds, and tracking how their function operates across five tiers: Alarm, Concern, Content, Effective, and Optimized.
faq.who_is_it_for.a: Organizations that want to own their performance management tooling outright. If you have the technical capacity to deploy and maintain a web application, you can operate Primer. It works best for teams that already maintain internal systems and want ownership over their tools, not vendor dependence.
faq.is_it_saas.a: No. Primer is sold as a perpetual source code license. You receive the complete source code, deploy it on your own infrastructure, and own it outright. There are no subscriptions, no per-seat fees, and no ongoing vendor dependency.
faq.what_do_i_get.q: What do I receive when I purchase?
faq.what_do_i_get.a: The complete source code as a downloadable package, deployment documentation, architecture guides, and configuration references. Everything you need to get the system running and understand how it works.
faq.how_much.a: A one-time perpetual license fee. No recurring charges. See the Pricing page for current pricing.
faq.updates.a: Yes - and honestly, they probably won't matter much to you. Primer is not a SaaS. You are not waiting on our roadmap. You own the source code, so the day you deploy you can move that button, change that color, or add that integration yourself instead of filing a feature request. License holders can download new versions from the dashboard whenever they want, but by the time we ship one, your codebase will have already diverged into something better suited to your team than anything we'd release.
faq.what_do_i_need.q: What do I need to deploy Primer?
faq.what_do_i_need.a: A PostgreSQL database and a hosting environment that can run a Node.js application. The system works with any PostgreSQL provider — Neon, Railway, self-hosted, or any PostgreSQL-compatible service.
faq.which_database.a: Any PostgreSQL-compatible provider. The codebase is provider-agnostic - there are no vendor-specific APIs or lock-in. You choose your infrastructure.
faq.can_i_modify.q: Can I modify the source code?
faq.can_i_modify.a: Yes. The code is yours. You can modify it to meet your needs, add features, change branding, integrate with your existing systems, or extend it however you see fit.
faq.do_you_offer_support.a: No, and that's by design. Ongoing support requires vendor access to customer systems, which reintroduces the data exposure and dependency that the perpetual license model eliminates. The code is yours - you operate it, maintain it, and modify it.
faq.what_if_something_breaks.a: You fix it, the same way you would with any internal system. The source code is fully documented with comprehensive inline comments, JSDoc annotations, and architecture guides. If you have developers who can maintain a web application, they can maintain Primer.
faq.deployment_help.q: Can you help with initial deployment?
faq.deployment_help.a: We sell source code, not services. The download includes detailed deployment documentation, database setup scripts, and a step-by-step guide. Most organizations with an IT team can deploy Primer themselves using the included instructions.
narrative.part.1.title: How Hans Built His Own Stack First
narrative.part.1.subtitle: Before asking anyone else, I had to do it myself.
narrative.part.1.content.0: I built my own quintile stack before I asked any of my senior leaders to do the same. It wasn't about setting an example. It was about understanding what I was asking of them.
narrative.part.1.content.1: The exercise forces you to get specific. What does 'Alarm' actually look like for your function? Not conceptually -- specifically. What number? What state? What would you see that would tell you something is critically wrong?
narrative.part.1.content.2: Two of my five metrics are currently at Concern. I'm not hiding that. My {metric_concern_1} isn't where I want it to be. Neither is {metric_concern_2}. But now I have language for what 'not where I want it' actually means -- and so does my team.
narrative.part.1.content.3: My composite score is 3.6. Effective. Not because everything is perfect. Because the things that matter most are performing, and the things that need attention have documented thresholds that tell us when we should act.
narrative.part.1.framing.ceo.intro: This is what you would build first.
narrative.part.1.framing.ceo.outro: Before you can ask your leaders to define their thresholds, you need to have done it yourself. Not to judge them. To understand what the exercise demands.
narrative.part.1.framing.vp.intro: This is what your executive leader would build before asking you to build yours. You would see their stack before they saw yours.
narrative.part.1.framing.vp.outro: Your CEO or executive has to go first. When they do, you'll see exactly what they're asking of you -- and you'll see that they held themselves to the same standard.
narrative.part.1.framing.team_lead.intro: This is the top-level view. Your leader's leader built this. Understanding why it exists helps you understand what yours connects to.
narrative.part.1.framing.team_lead.outro: The executive stack sets the context. Every metric you build in your own stack connects to something here. Knowing that helps you build yours with purpose.
narrative.part.2.title: The First Threshold Conversation
narrative.part.2.subtitle: Hans and Marcus define what a crisis actually looks like.
narrative.part.2.content.0: My first threshold conversation was with Marcus. I didn't open with Primer or quintiles or frameworks. I opened with a question: 'What are the three to five things that would keep you up at night if they went badly?'
narrative.part.2.content.1: Marcus answered. {marcus_metrics}. Standard operations leadership concerns. But then I asked the harder question: 'What does a crisis actually look like for each of those? Not conceptually. Specifically. What number? What state?'
narrative.part.2.content.2: That's when Marcus paused. He'd always known a crisis when he saw one. But making it explicit -- and having me agree to that definition in advance -- changed everything. It wasn't a performance review anymore. It was a shared standard we both owned.
narrative.part.2.content.3: His stack after that first conversation had two metrics with full threshold calibration. The other three had only Alarm and Optimized defined. The middle tiers were still pending. That's realistic. Nobody finishes this in one sitting.
narrative.part.2.framing.ceo.intro: This is your first conversation with your senior leader.
narrative.part.2.framing.ceo.outro: The threshold conversation isn't about evaluating performance. It's about agreeing on definitions before you need them.
narrative.part.2.framing.vp.intro: This is the conversation you would have with your executive. Marcus is in your seat. The question Hans asks him is the question your leader would ask you.
narrative.part.2.framing.vp.outro: The answer you give shapes everything that follows. The threshold definitions you create become the standard by which your work is measured -- and you wrote them.
narrative.part.2.framing.team_lead.intro: Watch what Marcus does here. He defines what a crisis looks like for his own function.
narrative.part.2.framing.team_lead.outro: That's the same exercise you would do with the leader above you. The definitions you create are yours. Your leader approves them, but you author them.
narrative.part.3.title: The Weight Conversation
narrative.part.3.subtitle: When Rachel realized her priorities had drifted.
narrative.part.3.content.0: Rachel's stack was fully built before this conversation. Her composite score was 3.8, Effective. Strong. But I had noticed something: she'd put 50% of her weight on budget variance.
narrative.part.3.content.1: I asked her about it. Not as a correction -- the weighting was defensible -- but as a question: 'If margin improvement is the strategic priority this quarter, does budget variance still deserve half your stack?'
narrative.part.3.content.2: Her answer surprised her. She'd weighted it that way because it was what she'd always tracked most closely, not because it was currently the most important thing. Habit had become strategy without anyone noticing.
narrative.part.3.content.3: The weight shifted. Her composite score recalculated. The operation hadn't changed. But the strategic framing had. This is the conversation I value most in the entire framework.
narrative.part.3.framing.ceo.intro: This is the kind of conversation you would initiate with your leaders.
narrative.part.3.framing.ceo.outro: Rachel's original weighting was defensible. The value wasn't in correcting her. It was in making a strategic assumption visible before it cost anything.
narrative.part.3.framing.vp.intro: This is a conversation about your stack. Rachel had a defensible weighting that no longer matched the strategic moment.
narrative.part.3.framing.vp.outro: When your leader reviews your weights, this is what the conversation looks like when it works. Not criticism. Discovery.
narrative.part.3.framing.team_lead.intro: Weight conversations happen between Rachel and Hans -- your equivalent and their leader.
narrative.part.3.framing.team_lead.outro: Understanding how weights get examined helps you build yours with intention from the start. Every weight is an assumption about priority.
narrative.part.4.title: Nina's Score Moves
narrative.part.4.subtitle: When the measurement was wrong, not the operation.
narrative.part.4.content.0: Nina's composite score stayed at Concern for two consecutive cycles. Her {nina_metric} threshold had been calibrated against an industry benchmark that no longer reflected our operational reality.
narrative.part.4.content.1: The benchmark assumed a crew mix and project type that matched our work five years ago. The threshold was generating a Concern designation that looked like a failure when it was a measurement problem.
narrative.part.4.content.2: Nina filed a self-inquiry at the quarterly cycle window. She presented the case for recalibration: new threshold, grounded in current data, reviewed and approved by me. Her score moved to Content the following month.
narrative.part.4.content.3: The score moved because the operation was being measured accurately, not because the operation improved. A Concern score that reflects reality is more valuable than a Content score built on a miscalibrated definition.
narrative.part.4.framing.ceo.intro: Nina brought this to you. You approved the recalibration.
narrative.part.4.framing.ceo.outro: The system gave Nina the mechanism to surface a measurement problem. The recalibration was documented, reviewed, and approved. That's how thresholds evolve.
narrative.part.4.framing.vp.intro: Nina is in your position. She identified a measurement problem and used the system to fix it.
narrative.part.4.framing.vp.outro: If your metrics are miscalibrated, this is how you surface it -- with data, through the system, not as a complaint in a meeting.
narrative.part.4.framing.team_lead.intro: This is the self-inquiry process. Watch how Nina handles a measurement problem.
narrative.part.4.framing.team_lead.outro: You have the same mechanism. If something is being measured wrong, document it and file the inquiry. The system is designed for this.
narrative.part.5.title: The Peer Inquiry
narrative.part.5.subtitle: When James's win rate depended on Rachel's lead quality.
narrative.part.5.content.0: James's win rate had been at Concern for six weeks. He'd traced the problem not to his team's performance but to the quality of leads being counted as qualified under Rachel's definition.
narrative.part.5.content.1: Leads meeting Rachel's definition were converting at 23%. James had set his win rate threshold assuming 40% conversion. The thresholds were built independently and had never been compared.
narrative.part.5.content.2: James filed a peer inquiry: target metric was Rachel's lead quality threshold, affected metric was his win rate, rationale was the conversion rate gap and the dependency between the two definitions.
narrative.part.5.content.3: The inquiry routed simultaneously to me and to Rachel. Neither was surprised by anything the other said when we met, because everything was already in writing. Two valid resolutions existed: tighten Rachel's threshold or recalibrate James's win rate to reflect reality. We chose to do both.
narrative.part.5.framing.ceo.intro: This inquiry routed to you. You facilitated the resolution.
narrative.part.5.framing.ceo.outro: The value isn't in which threshold changed. It's that the dependency is now visible and documented. These conversations happen constructively because they start with shared facts.
narrative.part.5.framing.vp.intro: James and Rachel are your peers. This is a mechanism for raising a cross-functional dependency without it becoming a political argument.
narrative.part.5.framing.vp.outro: You would file and receive these. The structure keeps it about metrics, not personalities.
narrative.part.5.framing.team_lead.intro: If a peer lead's metrics affect yours, this is the structured way to surface it.
narrative.part.5.framing.team_lead.outro: The inquiry documents the dependency so the conversation starts with shared facts. No ambush. No politics.
narrative.part.6.title: What Hans's Dashboard Shows Now
narrative.part.6.subtitle: The leadership view after a period of real use.
narrative.part.6.content.0: This is what I look at before every leadership meeting. Not to evaluate anyone. To know where to put the conversation.
narrative.part.6.content.1: All four of my senior leaders' composite scores are visible. Marcus's stack is still incomplete on two metrics -- that's fine, he's working through them. James's inquiry is resolved but his win rate hasn't yet recovered. Nina is at Content. Rachel is at Effective.
narrative.part.6.content.2: Every leader on my team built their own thresholds. Every leader weighted their own metrics. I reviewed and approved all of it. Nothing was imposed. The scores reflect definitions my leaders own.
narrative.part.6.content.3: This is the system after sustained use. Not a launch day screenshot. Real operations, real scores, real conversations captured.
narrative.part.6.framing.ceo.outro: The value isn't surveillance. It's context. You know where the conversation needs to go before you walk into the room.
narrative.part.6.framing.vp.intro: This is what your executive sees. Your composite score is one of these.
narrative.part.6.framing.vp.outro: Understanding this view helps you understand what your leader knows before they walk into the room with you.
narrative.part.6.framing.team_lead.intro: This is the executive view. You're not expected to see this in your deployment.
narrative.part.6.framing.team_lead.outro: But knowing it exists -- and knowing that it reflects thresholds you defined and weights you set -- changes how you think about the scores you own.
narrative.part.7.label: License
narrative.part.7.title: Own What You Deploy
narrative.part.7.subtitle: The license structure behind everything you just saw.
narrative.part.7.content.0: Every screen in this walkthrough runs on the same source code delivered with a Primer license. There is no hosted SaaS tier. No per-seat pricing. No dependency on {company_name}'s infrastructure remaining operational.
narrative.part.7.content.1: When you purchase a Primer license, you receive the complete source code. You deploy it on your own infrastructure -- Neon, Railway, self-hosted, or wherever else you run your stack. Tier has no access to your production environment because the architecture does not permit it.
narrative.part.7.content.2: This is a deliberate architectural choice, not a privacy policy. The system is designed so that the question of trusting Primer with your organization's performance data never arises. There is no channel through which that data could reach us.
narrative.part.7.content.3: The platform demonstration linked below shows the same codebase running against a 69-person fictional organization. You can explore it as any role in the org tree -- from the CEO to an individual contributor -- and see how the system behaves at each level.
narrative.part.7.framing.ceo.intro: As a CEO, you are deciding whether to put this system in your organization. The license structure means you are acquiring infrastructure, not subscribing to a service.
narrative.part.7.framing.ceo.outro: The platform demonstration below shows the system at scale. A 69-person org gives you a realistic sense of how scores, tiers, and inquiries behave under real operating conditions.
narrative.part.7.framing.vp.intro: As a VP, you will likely inherit this system from above. Understanding the license structure helps you set accurate expectations about what changes and what does not when the system is deployed.
narrative.part.7.framing.vp.outro: Explore the platform demonstration to see what your dashboard would look like and how inquiries work at your level of the org.
narrative.part.7.framing.team_lead.intro: As a team lead, the license model mostly means the tool is owned by your organization -- which means your organization controls how it runs, not a vendor.
narrative.part.7.framing.team_lead.outro: The platform demonstration lets you explore the system from any level of the org tree, including from the perspective of someone at your level.

audit.title: Audit Log
tooltip.stack.add_metric: Define 4–5 metrics that measure your team's operational health. Each needs a name, weight (all must total 100%), and threshold descriptions for every tier.
tooltip.stack.thresholds: Write observable conditions for each tier. 'Alarm' names the exact trigger condition, not 'behind schedule' but the specific failure mode. This language is reviewed and agreed on with your superior before it is locked.
tooltip.stack.weight: Weights reflect current priorities. The composite score is a weighted average of your metric tiers. All weights must sum to 100%.
tooltip.stack.submit: Submit sends this metric to your superior for review. Finalize threshold language beforehand, it cannot be changed after approval. The approved definition becomes the shared reference for evaluating your performance.
tooltip.stack.edit: Edit the metric name, weight, and threshold descriptions. Changes are tracked in the audit log.
tooltip.performance.log: Record actual measured values from your data source. Entries cannot be edited once saved, this protects the integrity of your operational record.
tooltip.performance.cadence: How often you measure this metric. Period dates auto-fill from your selection. Each metric can have an independent cadence.
tooltip.goals.add: Goals connect strategy to team work. Cascaded goals from your manager show their origin. Self-created goals capture your own initiatives.
tooltip.goals.cascade: Sends this goal to all your direct reports. Each copy carries its origin, goal_origin is set to 'cascaded' and the source chain is preserved up the hierarchy. If this goal was itself cascaded to you, the full lineage remains visible. Track acceptance, deferrals, and pending status per report.
tooltip.goals.dependency: Link goals with blocks, informs, or supports relationships. Makes cross-team dependencies visible in both goal lists.
tooltip.inquiries.overview: Self-inquiries challenge your own metric, on cycle only. Peer inquiries can be filed any time when another team measurably impacts your metrics. When an inquiry stalls, the org admin can step in to resolve or dismiss it without being the named authority.
tooltip.inquiries.type: Self: challenge your own metric's threshold, weight, or definition. Peer: challenge a peer's metric that is impacting your own stack.
tooltip.inquiries.affected_metric: Name the metric in your own stack that is being impacted. You must show your stake, this requirement prevents frivolous challenges.
tooltip.inquiries.resolve: Resolve closes the inquiry with a recorded outcome. Dismiss closes it without a formal decision. Either action is final, the inquiry cannot be reopened. Only the named authority or an org admin can resolve or dismiss.
tooltip.leaders.assign: Assign a metric to this team member. It appears labeled 'Assigned' in their stack, they define threshold language and submit for your review. Use 'Co-authored' when you defined the metric together; it appears with a distinct label and reflects joint ownership rather than a top-down mandate.
tooltip.leaders.co_authored: Co-authored means the metric definition was a genuine collaboration, the team member had meaningful input into what each tier looks like. This distinction matters: co-authored thresholds tend to produce better calibration than assigned ones because the person who lives the metric helped define it.
tooltip.leaders.snapshot: Recording a metric review locks the current metric stack and records the composite score for this cycle. Record at the end of each review period. Metrics cannot be edited until you start a new cycle.
tooltip.leaders.new_cycle: Unlocks the metric stack after a metric review so the team member can update tier assessments and begin the next measurement period.
tooltip.leaders.approve: Approving locks this threshold language as the shared operational reference for the cycle. Before you approve, confirm the descriptions match your own definition of each tier, this is the calibration conversation. Once approved, neither party can change the thresholds until the next cycle inquiry.
tooltip.leaders.snapshot_adjust: Amend a metric review when a post-quarter review surfaces context that changes the recorded assessment. The original record is preserved, the entry shows who amended it, when, and the reason you provide. Use this sparingly: routine use undermines the integrity of the operational record.
leaders.co_authored_help: Check if this metric was developed collaboratively with the employee. Co-authored metrics are labeled differently in their stack to reflect shared ownership.
goals.adjustment_reason_placeholder: Why is this goal being updated?
goals.adjustment_reason_help: Optional but recommended. All changes are logged regardless.
team.snapshot_success: Metric review recorded and metrics locked.
tooltip.goals.adjustment_log: Every edit to this goal is recorded here, field changed, previous value, new value, who made the change, and their stated reason. Entries are immutable. No goal can be silently modified; the paper trail is automatic regardless of whether a reason is provided.
tooltip.team.snapshot: Record individual metric reviews for your direct reports from this page. Each review locks that person's metric stack and records their composite score for the cycle. You can also record from each person's detail page.
tooltip.reports.score_trend: Your composite score is a weighted average of all your metric tiers, plotted over each measurement period. A stable trend confirms consistent execution. A declining trend is your signal to investigate, use the breakdown below to isolate which metrics are dragging.
tooltip.reports.metric_breakdown: Each metric's tier history shown independently. Use this to isolate which areas are improving or declining regardless of your overall composite. A metric that improved while your composite declined means something else dropped, weights tell you what matters most.
tooltip.reports.tier_distribution: How your metric assessments have distributed across the five tiers over all recorded periods. A concentration at Alarm or Concern signals sustained structural problems. A spread across Effective and Optimized confirms broad operational health.
tooltip.reports.team_overview: Your direct reports ranked by current composite score. Sparklines show each person's trajectory, a declining sparkline is the earliest signal of an emerging problem before it reaches the inquiry stage.
tooltip.peers.overview: Peers are other leaders under the same authority as you. What you can see depends on the visibility level your authority has set, score only, metrics, or full stack. If a peer's metric is measurably affecting yours, this is where you identify the target before filing a peer inquiry.
tooltip.admin.pending_placements: People who have joined the organization but are not yet placed in the hierarchy. Until placed, they can see the system but cannot participate, no metrics, no inquiries. Resolve the request directly or escalate to their hiring manager.
tooltip.admin.audit_log: Browse the append-only change history for this organization, every metric update, threshold revision, inquiry action, and access change, each with a timestamp and the actor who made it. Entries cannot be modified or deleted.
tooltip.admin.compliance_export: Downloads the full audit log as a CSV. Suitable for external auditors or internal compliance reviews. The data reflects exactly what happened, no edits, no omissions.
tooltip.visibility.peer_visibility: Controls how much your direct reports see of each other's metric stacks. Score Only shows the composite tier only. Metrics exposes names and tiers. Full grants complete stack access including threshold descriptions.
tooltip.visibility.grants: Grants give a specific person elevated access to a subtree or the entire organization, outside their normal hierarchy position. Use this for HR, Chief of Staff, or auditors who need cross-hierarchy visibility without a management role.
problem.title: The Problem
problem.hero_sub: The system designed to improve performance actively damages it.
problem.section1.heading: Reviews damage performance.
problem.section1.stat1: 95% of managers are frustrated with their review systems.
problem.section1.stat2: Reviews make performance worse one-third of the time.
problem.section1.stat3: Only 2% of Fortune 500 CHROs say their system inspires improvement.
problem.section1.body: A company with 10,000 employees spends roughly $35 million per year conducting reviews. Managers spend 210 hours per year on performance management activities. The return on that investment is negative.
problem.section2.heading: Middle managers feel invisible.
problem.section2.stat1: 82% of middle managers report feeling invisible and frustrated.
problem.section2.stat2: 71% of HR leaders admit they are not developing mid-level leaders.
problem.section2.stat3: 34% of employees say they never want a management role.
problem.section2.body: Organizations claim they empower mid-level leaders. The leaders disagree. Permission alone is not empowerment when executive leaders are not actually vesting authority.
problem.section3.heading: Goals are imposed, not owned.
problem.section3.stat1: Only 50% of employees know what is expected of them at work.
problem.section3.body: When KPIs are imposed top-down without involving the people expected to meet them, the result is widespread dissatisfaction, low morale, and high turnover.
problem.section4.heading: Silos create invisible damage.
problem.section4.stat1: Managers account for 70% of the variance in team engagement.
problem.section4.stat2: 61% of senior managers say at least half of decision-making time is ineffective.
problem.section4.body: Each department creates its own dashboard. This produces conflicting truths. The dependency between one leader's metric and another leader's metric remains invisible until it causes damage.
problem.section5.heading: Metrics go stale. No one fixes them.
problem.section5.body: KPIs that are not regularly reviewed become outdated. There is no structured, recurring process for leaders to challenge whether their metrics still make sense. The result is rigidity followed by crisis-driven transformation.
problem.section6.heading: You wait for someone else's priorities.
problem.section6.body: Most enterprise software ships features the vendor's product team prioritized, not yours. Your specific need joins a backlog of thousands. The lucky requests arrive in eighteen to twenty-four months. Most never arrive at all. Decades of research find that roughly two-thirds of software features are rarely or never used by the customers paying for them.
problem.section6.source: Standish Group, CHAOS Report; Pendo, Feature Adoption Report (2019)
problem.cta_heading: What if there was a different way?
problem.cta_body: A framework where leaders define their own metrics. Where priorities are explicit. Where cross-functional conflicts have a formal resolution process. Where you own the system, permanently.
how.title: How It Works
how.hero_sub: Primer replaces subjective performance reviews with a structured system where employees participate in defining how they're measured.
how.framework_body: Every metric is measured against five tiers. Not pass/fail. Not a score from 1 to 10. Five meaningful states that tell you exactly where you stand.
how.pillar1.heading: Own Your Metrics
how.pillar1.body: Critical and legally required metrics are set top-down by management. Employees then participate: they define the weighting across all their metrics and propose additional goals of their own. Managers review and approve the complete picture. No one operates under a scorecard they had no hand in shaping.
how.pillar2.heading: Weight What Matters
how.pillar2.body: Not everything is equally important. Employees assign explicit weights to each metric. When you say safety is 40% and schedule is 30%, you have made a strategic statement.
how.pillar3.heading: See Your Score
how.pillar3.body: Composite scores are calculated transparently. You can see exactly how each metric contributes to your overall tier. No black boxes.
how.pillar4.heading: Challenge the System
how.pillar4.body: The Inquiry mechanism lets any employee formally challenge a peer's metric, threshold, or weight. Cross-functional conflicts get resolved through structure, not politics.
how.pillar5.heading: Cascade Goals
how.pillar5.body: Strategic goals flow down the organization with full dependency tracking. Every goal connects to the metrics that measure its success.

pricing.includes.roadmap: No waiting on someone else's roadmap
pricing.roadmap_sub: The biggest reason free updates barely matter: you stopped waiting for them.
pricing.roadmap_saas_body: You file a feature request and join a queue of thousands. A product manager you'll never meet decides if it ships. Six months later the status is still 'Future Consideration.' Public trackers at major SaaS vendors still hold tickets filed in 2017, with hundreds of votes and no answer.
pricing.roadmap_primer_body: You own the source code on day one. Move buttons, change colors, add integrations, yourself, today. Pull our updates when they're useful. Ignore them when they're not. By the time we ship a release, your version already fits your team better than ours ever could.
pricing.tco_sub: How Primer compares to a typical SaaS subscription for a 50-person team.
pricing.philosophy_body: SaaS is a liability. You pay monthly. The moment you stop paying, you lose access. Every dollar spent on SaaS is gone the day the subscription ends. A perpetual source code license is an asset. You pay once. The code is yours. The value compounds instead of draining.
pricing.faq1.a: A zip file containing the complete source code. TypeScript, SvelteKit, PostgreSQL schema, all 9 language files, documentation, and deployment guides.
pricing.faq2.a: Yes, and they're free for the life of the product - but they probably won't matter much. This is not a SaaS where you wait on our roadmap. You own the source code, so the day you deploy you start changing things: moving buttons, adjusting colors, adding integrations. By the time we ship an update, your version will have diverged into something that fits your team better than ours. You can pull our changes from the dashboard whenever you want, or ignore them entirely.
pricing.faq3.a: Completely. Change the branding, add features, integrate with your systems. The source code is yours.
pricing.faq4.a: Your deployment continues to work. You own the source code. Your system has no dependency on our infrastructure.
pricing.faq5.q: Is this really not a SaaS?
pricing.faq5.a: Correct. There is no hosted version. No per-seat fees. No usage-based charges. You deploy and run the software yourself. You own the source code.
pricing.tco_footnote: At Year 3, a SaaS tool has cost you $54,000 and counting. Primer is still that same $5,000 you paid three years ago.
pricing.tco_disclaimer: * SaaS estimate based on $30/user/month for 50 users

security.answer_body: Every security question has the same answer. You can audit every line. You can change anything. Nothing phones home. Nothing is hidden.

about.title: About DavidPM
about.hero_sub: DavidPM is the company behind Primer. We believe business software should be an asset you own, not a subscription you rent.
about.thesis_p1: The future of business software is ownership. AI-assisted development means sophisticated tools are more accessible than ever. What organizations need is a well-built foundation they can adapt, not another cloud platform they depend on.
about.thesis_p2: Primer exists because performance management is broken and the tools built to fix it are part of the problem. SaaS vendors profit from dependency. We profit from building something worth owning.
about.thesis_p3: The relationship ends at the point of purchase. What you do with the product is up to you. We don't want ongoing control over your operations. We want you to succeed on your own terms.
about.contact_body: Questions about Primer? Interested in a license? We'd love to hear from you.
license.hero_sub: Every question about what you can do with Primer has a simple answer: you own the source code.
license.summary_body: You pay $5,000 once. You receive the complete source code. You deploy it wherever you want. You modify it however you want. You use it for as long as you want. That's it.
how.canvas_heading: We don't have to be right.
how.canvas_body: You're not relying on us to fix your organization. Primer is a finished, working system. Deploy it and use it today. If your culture, language, or way of working ever calls for changes, you own the source code and can adapt any of it on your own schedule. Most teams run it as shipped. Some reshape it over time. Both are valid; neither is required to get value on day one.
pricing.canvas_body: Primer ships as a complete, working product. Install it and it runs. You don't have to reshape anything to start getting value. And if your organization has a different vocabulary, a different visual style, or existing systems to plug into, you own the source code and can change any of it whenever it suits you. Ready on day one. Yours to evolve on your own schedule.

onboarding.title: Get Primer running the way you run
onboarding.subtitle: Three short paths. Pick the one that matches where your team is today, each one ends with you having done something, not just read something.
onboarding.principle1.title: We don't have to be right
onboarding.principle1.body: Every word, tier, and metric in Primer is a draft. You know your organization better than we ever will, rename anything, rewrite anything, delete anything that doesn't fit.
onboarding.principle2.title: You don't have to wait for features
onboarding.principle2.body: The source code is yours. If Primer doesn't do what you need, you can change it, or have a developer change it, without asking anyone or waiting for a roadmap.
onboarding.admin_only_note: Only CEOs and system admins see this tab. When your team is up and running, you can remove it entirely, instructions are in the code.
onboarding.needs_placement: You haven't been placed in the organizational hierarchy yet. Let your manager know so they can add you and you can start defining your metrics.
onboarding.request_sent: Request sent. Your manager will place you soon.
onboarding.complete_message: Here's what we'd do next, if this were our org.
onboarding.rollout.title: Next: roll this out to your team
onboarding.rollout.body: Invite your direct reports, let each of them author their own tier definitions, and hold exactly one group check-in together before you leave them alone. Don't try to govern the rubric from the top, the whole point is that every level writes its own. You're setting the tone, not the content.
onboarding.path1.title: We're starting from scratch
onboarding.path1.description: Pick a starter template, edit it to match how your team actually talks about work, and leave with your first three metrics drafted.
onboarding.path1.step1.title: Pick a starter template, or start blank
onboarding.path1.step1.body:
Templates are drafts, not prescriptions. Pick one that's roughly close to your business and change everything that doesn't fit. If none of them feel right, start blank, you'll spend the same 15 minutes either way, and the template just saves you typing.

The only rule: don't treat the starter content as sacred. It isn't.
onboarding.path1.step2.title: Meet the tier model, and rename it if you want to
onboarding.path1.step2.body:
Primer ships with five tiers: Alarm, Concern, Content, Effective, Optimized. We chose these labels because they describe states instead of numbers, 'what shape is this in?' is a more useful weekly question than 'did we hit the target?'

But those are just our words. If your team would respond better to Red / Yellow / Green / Great / Gold, or to words in your own language, or to a scale you've used for years, rename them. The model is the five slots; the labels are yours.
onboarding.path1.step3.title: Draft three metrics, one sentence per tier
onboarding.path1.step3.body:
Don't overthink this. Write fast, write rough, and ship a first version you'll want to edit in a week. That's the point, the rubric is a living document, and nothing improves until it's been through one real check-in.

Three metrics is the target. Two is fine. Five is too many for a first pass, even if you're sure.
onboarding.path1.step4.title: Put it on the wall for a week before you touch it
onboarding.path1.step4.body:
Resist the urge to polish the rubric before you've run a single check-in against it. You need real data, one week of tier updates, to know what actually needs rewriting. Polishing before you use it is where most first attempts quietly die.

Come back in seven days and edit freely. Until then, leave it alone.
onboarding.path2.title: We've tried goals and they haven't stuck
onboarding.path2.description: Diagnose what actually broke last time, cut your current list to something honest, and leave with a 30-day reset plan you can run without asking anyone's permission.
onboarding.path2.duration: ~15 min · ends with a shorter, honest list
onboarding.path2.step1.title: Why the last attempt didn't stick
onboarding.path2.step1.body:
"Going through the motions" is usually one of two things, and they need different fixes:

1) Disengagement, people knew what to do, stopped caring, and the goals quietly died. This is a culture and leadership problem, not a process problem.

2) Bad execution, people cared, but the goals were vanity metrics, or the data wasn't there, or the list was too long to track. This is a design problem, not a people problem.

Most teams have both, but one dominates. The next step helps you figure out which.
onboarding.path2.step2.title: Diagnose which flavor you're in
onboarding.path2.step2.body:
Ask yourself honestly. The first three questions diagnose disengagement:

• Did leadership talk about goals at all in the last month?
• Could every IC name their team's top metric without looking it up?
• When a goal was missed, did anything actually change?

If most answers are no, the fix is leadership modeling, not more process.

The next three diagnose bad execution:

• Could you actually measure the things you set as goals?
• Did your KRs describe outcomes, or tasks that felt like outcomes?
• Did you set three goals, or eight?

If most answers point to broken goals, the fix is cutting the list and rewriting what's left. Both paths start with the next step, which is subtraction.
onboarding.path2.step3.title: The reset, keep three, cut everything else
onboarding.path2.step3.body:
This is a deletion exercise, not a rebuild. Open your current goal list, pick the three that would matter most if the others disappeared tomorrow, and archive everything else. Don't rewrite anything yet. Just cut.

The fewer goals, the more attention each one gets. Teams running three goals consistently outperform teams running seven, even when the seven are individually better written. Focus is the entire point.
onboarding.path2.step4.title: A 30-day plan you can actually run
onboarding.path2.step4.body:
Week 1, Archive. Cut the list to three. Don't rewrite anything.
Week 2, Rewrite the three survivors using tier descriptions. One sentence per tier.
Week 3, Run your first honest check-in. No backfilling missed weeks, missed check-ins are data, not failures.
Week 4, Retrospective. Keep what worked. Rewrite what didn't. Delete anything that still feels wrong.

Commit to at least three cycles before you judge the framework. The first cycle is always messy. That's the cycle where you learn what 'good' looks like for your team, not the cycle where you prove the framework works.
onboarding.path3.title: I know what I'm doing, help me make this ours
onboarding.path3.description: Skip the theory. Tour the editable surfaces, see how Primer composes with the OKRs and KPIs you already run, and leave with something personalized and a brief you can share with your exec team.
onboarding.path3.step1.title: Everything you see is editable
onboarding.path3.step1.body:
Primer names, metric descriptions, cadences, report layouts, navigation, even this onboarding tab, all of it lives in the source code you were shipped. You don't have to wait for features. If something doesn't fit, change it, or have a developer change it, without asking anyone.

This is the part of Primer most teams underuse. Start small: rename one tier, rewrite one metric, delete one field you don't need. The product bends where you push it.
onboarding.path3.step2.title: How Primer composes with the stack you already run
onboarding.path3.step2.body:
Primer is not a replacement for OKRs or KPIs. It's a different layer on top of them:

• OKRs describe aspiration, where do we want to be by quarter end?
• KPIs describe measurement, what number are we tracking today?
• Primer describes state, what shape is this metric in, and what's a good next move?

You can run all three. Most of our sharper customers do. Primer gives you a shared vocabulary for the weekly conversation; OKRs give you the quarterly target; KPIs give you the number behind it. Nothing has to be thrown out.
onboarding.path3.step3.title: Make your first customization
onboarding.path3.step3.body:
Pick one thing in Primer that feels wrong for your team and change it. Rename a tier. Rewrite a starter metric. Delete a field you don't use. Adjust the cadence. It genuinely doesn't matter which one, the exercise is proving to yourself that the system bends to you, not the other way around.

If you can't get it to bend the way you want from the UI, the source code is yours. Every assumption in Primer is a file you can open.
onboarding.path3.step4.title: What to tell your exec team
onboarding.path3.step4.body:
A short brief you can adapt for your next leadership meeting. Rewrite anything that doesn't sound like you.

---

"We're adopting Primer as our goal system. It's a tier-based model, every metric gets described in five states instead of a number, so the weekly conversation moves from 'did we hit the target' to 'what shape is this in, and what moves it up a tier.'

It composes with the OKRs and KPIs we already run. Everything in it is editable and the source code is ours, if it doesn't fit, we change it. We're committing to three cycles before we judge the framework, and the first cycle will be messy on purpose, that's the cycle where we learn what 'good' looks like for us."

---

Edit this. It's your voice, not ours.

pricing.license_scope.title: One license = one legal entity

If you operate multiple legal entities, a PE firm's portfolio companies, a consultant's client engagements, a holding company's subsidiaries, each one needs its own $5,000 license. The rule is simple: if the data belongs to a separate company, that company needs its own license.

consultants.license.title: The licensing rule (read this first)
consultants.license.body: One perpetual source-code license covers one legal entity. The organization that will use Primer buys the license directly. You recommend it, configure it, and deploy it — but the license belongs to the organization, not the consultant.
consultants.license.rule1: If you run Primer internally for your consulting practice and no client data is ever stored in it: one license covers your practice.
consultants.license.rule2: The moment you deploy it for a client and that client's employee data lives in it, that deployment needs its own license.
consultants.license.rule3: Three clients, three separate deployments, three separate companies' data: three licenses. Each is $5,000, perpetual, owned by the organization.
consultants.license.rule4: A PE operating partner running Primer across 12 portcos: 12 licenses. One per portco.
consultants.license.why_body: Primer is priced to make this easy. At $5,000 per legal entity, perpetual, no recurring fees, unlimited users, it's meant to fit inside an engagement fee or a capital-expense line, not to be multiplied across companies on a single seat. Mixing multiple clients' data into one deployment breaks the data-sovereignty model customers buy Primer for in the first place.

consultants.faq.title: Common questions
consultants.faq.q1: Can I buy one license and use it for multiple clients?
consultants.faq.a1: No. One license per legal entity whose data lives in the deployment. If three clients' employee data is in three separate installs, you need three licenses. The $5,000 price is set so this works out economically inside a consulting engagement.
consultants.faq.q2: Who buys and owns the license?
consultants.faq.a2: The organization that will use Primer. The license is purchased by and belongs to the legal entity whose employee data lives in the deployment. You recommend and deploy Primer, but the organization buys and owns it.
consultants.faq.q3: Can I rebrand Primer as my own product?
consultants.faq.a3: You can rebrand it for each client deployment, their logo, their colors, their wording. You cannot re-sell Primer as your own SaaS product or redistribute the source code. The license is for deployment, not repackaging.
consultants.faq.q4: Do I need technical staff to deploy it?
consultants.faq.a4: If you can run a Docker command, yes. The download includes step-by-step deployment documentation, database setup scripts, and a plain-language walkthrough. Most consultants with basic IT experience can deploy Primer for a client in under an hour.

security.frameworks.title: Compliance framework mapping
security.frameworks.intro: Compliance teams will ask how Primer fits specific frameworks. Here is our defensible position on each of the major ones. The short version: Primer runs inside your compliance perimeter, not outside it.
security.frameworks.stance.title: Our stance
security.frameworks.stance.body: DavidPM does not hold certifications under any of these frameworks, and we believe those certifications would be the wrong tool for a self-hosted source-code product. A certification would cover our office; it would not cover your deployment. Instead, we deliver source code and an architecture that lets your existing compliance program cover Primer directly, the same way it already covers any internal application your team runs.
security.frameworks.hipaa.name: HIPAA
security.frameworks.hipaa.question: Is Primer HIPAA-compliant?
security.frameworks.hipaa.answer: HIPAA compliance attaches to the deployment, not to the software. Primer runs on your infrastructure and DavidPM never receives, stores, or transmits PHI. There is no data flow between your deployment and DavidPM, so no Business Associate Agreement with DavidPM is required or possible, we are not a Business Associate under the HIPAA Privacy Rule. You deploy Primer inside your existing HIPAA-covered environment the same way you deploy any internally hosted application.
security.frameworks.hipaa.yours: Access controls, audit logging, encryption at rest and in transit, BAAs with your own hosting and infrastructure providers, workforce training, and breach notification procedures.
security.frameworks.gdpr.name: GDPR / DSGVO
security.frameworks.gdpr.question: How does Primer handle GDPR?
security.frameworks.gdpr.answer: Under GDPR and the German DSGVO, your organization is the data controller for the employee data in Primer, and your organization is also the processor because Primer runs on infrastructure you operate. DavidPM is neither a controller nor a processor of that data, we never receive it. No cross-border transfer to DavidPM occurs, so Standard Contractual Clauses for DavidPM are not applicable. Data residency is whatever you choose when you deploy. EU-only or Germany-only deployment is straightforward: install on infrastructure inside the jurisdiction.
security.frameworks.gdpr.yours: Your Article 30 records of processing, DPIA if required, lawful basis for processing employee data, data subject request handling, and works council consultation where applicable.
security.frameworks.soc2.name: SOC 2
security.frameworks.soc2.question: What is Primer's SOC 2 scope?
security.frameworks.soc2.answer: Primer is not a service you consume from DavidPM, so it does not fall inside a DavidPM SOC 2 report, and a DavidPM SOC 2 report would not cover your deployment even if one existed. Your Primer deployment is inside your SOC 2 scope, not ours. If your organization already holds SOC 2 Type II, Primer becomes another internally managed application under your existing Trust Services Criteria. Auditors who understand self-hosted software accept this model.
security.frameworks.soc2.yours: Change management, access reviews, incident response, backup and recovery, and the rest of your existing SOC 2 controls applied to Primer as an in-scope system.
security.frameworks.fedramp.name: FedRAMP / NIST 800-53
security.frameworks.fedramp.question: Can Primer be used in FedRAMP-authorized environments?
security.frameworks.fedramp.answer: Primer itself is not FedRAMP-authorized, DavidPM is not a cloud service provider, so there is no service to authorize. Primer can be deployed inside a FedRAMP Moderate or High environment (for example, a GovCloud tenancy or an on-premise ATO boundary) as a customer-developed application. Because the source code is delivered directly, your security team can perform the same SA-11 developmental security testing and SA-15 development process review they would perform on any internally developed application. The NIST 800-53 control families that apply to Primer are the ones that apply to any application running inside your authorization boundary.
security.frameworks.fedramp.yours: ATO for the deployment environment, control inheritance decisions, continuous monitoring, POA&M management, and integration with your agency's existing authorization boundary.
security.frameworks.airgap.name: Air-gap deployment
security.frameworks.airgap.question: Does Primer run on an air-gapped network?
security.frameworks.airgap.answer: Yes. Primer has no phone-home behavior, no external API calls, no license check against a DavidPM server, and no required outbound network access of any kind once installed. The application does not fetch fonts, scripts, analytics, or dependencies from the internet at runtime. All assets ship inside the source code package. Once installed on an isolated network, Primer operates without any connection to the outside world, permanently. Updates are distributed as downloadable source packages that you transfer across the air gap using whatever procedures your environment requires.
security.frameworks.airgap.yours: Transfer procedures for source code updates across the air gap, offline dependency mirroring if you rebuild from source, and verification of package integrity against published checksums.
security.frameworks.sbom.name: SBOM / supply chain
security.frameworks.sbom.question: Is a Software Bill of Materials available?
security.frameworks.sbom.answer: Yes. A CycloneDX-format SBOM is included in every release and lists every direct and transitive dependency, its version, and its license. Because you receive the complete source code, you can also generate your own SBOM at any time using standard tooling (syft, cdxgen, npm sbom) and verify it against ours. You are not dependent on DavidPM for supply-chain visibility, the package.json, lockfile, and every line of code are in your hands from the moment of purchase. This aligns with the US Executive Order 14028 expectations for SBOM delivery in procured software.
security.frameworks.sbom.yours: Regular SBOM regeneration after any dependency updates you apply, vulnerability monitoring against your SBOM using your preferred tooling, and integration with your existing supply-chain security program.
security.frameworks.not_claim.title: What we do not claim
security.frameworks.not_claim.body: Primer is not certified under any compliance framework. DavidPM does not hold SOC 2 Type II, FedRAMP authorization, ISO 27001, HITRUST, or HIPAA attestations, and we do not offer a DavidPM-signed Business Associate Agreement, Data Processing Addendum, or security questionnaire response as a standard artifact. If a vendor-held certification is a hard requirement for your procurement process, Primer may not be the right product for your organization. If instead your compliance program is built to cover internally deployed software, which is how every HIPAA-covered hospital, every FedRAMP-authorized agency, and every SOC 2 organization already runs most of its internal tools, Primer fits that model cleanly.
how.two_party.heading: Two-party metric ownership
how.two_party.body1: Most performance tools let a single person, usually the manager, sometimes HR, define both what gets measured and what counts as good. That's how review cycles get political, and it's why so many scorecards feel like something done to people instead of with them.
how.two_party.body2: Primer splits the responsibility. Critical and legally required metrics are set top-down by management — but employees participate by defining the weighting and proposing additional goals of their own. Managers review and approve the complete picture. Neither party can finalize a scorecard alone, and mid-cycle adjustments require a documented reason plus a second-party approval, so a bad quarter cannot be quietly rewritten as a good one. The conversation is built into the data model, and the audit trail is permanent.
how.two_party.step1.label: Top-down
how.two_party.step1.title: Management sets critical metrics
how.two_party.step1.body: Legally required and strategically critical metrics are defined at the organizational level and assigned to each role.
how.two_party.step2.label: Bottom-up
how.two_party.step2.title: Employees participate and shape
how.two_party.step2.body: Employees define the weighting, propose additional goals, and shape how success is measured for their role. Managers review and approve the complete scorecard.

resources.mg.shared.three_paths_intro: None of these ask you to give anything up. Pick the lowest cost option you can get away with. Your own data will tell you what fits.
resources.mg.shared.mods_footer: You bought a perpetual source license. Every part of Primer is yours to change. These are suggestions, not requirements.
resources.mg.index.title: Bring the goal system you already run into Primer
resources.mg.index.subtitle: Respectful translation guides for the frameworks teams already trust.
resources.mg.index.stance_eyebrow: This is not a replace-your-stack page
resources.mg.index.stance_body: If OKRs, KPI dashboards, Balanced Scorecard, 4DX, EOS, Hoshin Kanri, or MBO work for your team, they work. Every guide below shows how to run Primer alongside the system you already run, concurrently, combined, or in an A/B test, and then lets your team make the call.
resources.mg.index.helper_heading: Not sure which guide to read?
resources.mg.index.helper_intro: The guides are organized by source system. Pick the one closest to how your team talks about goals today.
resources.mg.index.helper_q1_body: If you grade objectives on a 0–1 scale or publish a KPI dashboard, start with the OKRs or KPIs guide.
resources.mg.index.helper_q2_body: If you already practice Balanced Scorecard, 4DX, EOS / Traction, or Hoshin Kanri, use the matching guide.
resources.mg.index.helper_q3_body: If your team grew up on Management by Objectives or Key Result Areas, those guides are the gentlest entry points.
