resources.mg.okrs.title: Running OKRs alongside Primer
resources.mg.okrs.subtitle: OKRs got you here. Primer can run beside them, not instead of them.
resources.mg.okrs.summary: Keep your Objectives and Key Results. Mirror them into Primer's goal + metric stack for one cycle and compare what each model tells you.
resources.mg.okrs.stance: OKRs work. They are why your team has a shared vocabulary for ambition in the first place, and the discipline of setting quarterly KRs is genuinely hard won. This guide is not going to argue otherwise. What it will do is show how to translate the OKR shape you already use into Primer's goal, metric, and threshold model so you can run both for a cycle and let your own scoring data settle the question.
resources.mg.okrs.concepts_intro: Five rows cover the core of any OKR system. Every Primer concept named below is already built in. No schema changes needed.
resources.mg.okrs.row1_note: Title and description carry the aspiration; due date carries the cycle.
resources.mg.okrs.row2_note: Cleanest route for 'move this number' KRs, thresholds encode the achievement bands.
resources.mg.okrs.row3_dst: child goal linked as a supporting dependency
resources.mg.okrs.row3_note: Cleanest route for 'ship this thing' KRs, the dependency graph captures the link.
resources.mg.okrs.row4_dst: cascaded goal with an accept or defer workflow
resources.mg.okrs.row4_note: Stricter than most OKR tools, a superior's KR is traceable down-tree and accepted or deferred in writing.
resources.mg.okrs.row5_dst: the resolution workflow plus the five tier rubric (alarm to optimized)
resources.mg.okrs.row5_note: Tiers replace 0.0–1.0 scores with a rubric leaders read at a glance.
resources.mg.okrs.concurrent_heading: Run Primer concurrently for one cycle
resources.mg.okrs.concurrent_body: Keep your OKR tool as the source of record. For one quarter, mirror the same Objectives and KRs into Primer as goals and metrics. Score both at the end of the cycle. Compare what each view said about the state of the business. Most teams find that the five-tier rubric matches what their skip-level meetings already conclude anyway.
resources.mg.okrs.combine_body: Instead of running two systems in parallel, fold your OKRs into Primer directly: strategic objectives become strategic goals, numeric KRs become metrics with tiered thresholds, and initiative KRs become dependent supports goals. Your OKR vocabulary is preserved; the underlying model gains weight, cadence, origin metadata, and an audit trail.
resources.mg.okrs.ab_heading: A/B test for a single cycle
resources.mg.okrs.ab_body: Split one team's quarter. Half of the KRs get graded the OKR way (0.0–1.0 scoring); half get committed inside Primer with tiered thresholds and the accept/defer workflow. At quarter-end, ask the team which set of conversations was more useful, and let them keep whichever workflow they pick.
resources.mg.okrs.additions_heading: What Primer contributes beyond the standard OKR model
resources.mg.okrs.additions_intro: OKRs travel light by design. Primer carries extra metadata, none of it required, all of it optional to turn on. Here is what you gain if you do.
resources.mg.okrs.addition1_body: Primer scores every metric against five named bands (alarm → concern → content → effective → optimized). Stakeholders stop debating whether 0.68 is 'good' and start reading health. You can still grade OKRs the old way; tiers are a second view.
resources.mg.okrs.addition2_body: Metrics carry a declared weight that must sum to 100. Priorities are stated, not implied, the thing the team cares most about is the thing with the biggest number next to it.
resources.mg.okrs.addition3_body: lead or lag typing distinguishes the signal you can watch today (leading) from the number that lands next quarter (lagging). OKRs don't make that distinction; Primer encodes it.
resources.mg.okrs.addition4_body: The resolution workflow (draft, aligned, leader accepted, superior accepted, committed) forces the target setting negotiation OKRs assume but never model. Once the cycle is committed and the snapshot is locked, targets freeze. No mid cycle goalpost moving, the single most common OKR failure mode.
resources.mg.okrs.addition5_body: the audit log logs field, old value, new value, and reason on every change. Most OKR tools don't retain this; Primer keeps it for the life of the cycle.
resources.mg.okrs.modifications_heading: Customization suggestions, code you may want to modify
resources.mg.okrs.modifications_intro: You own the source. If your team is used to OKRs and you want Primer to feel native, here are changes customers commonly make. Each one is optional.
resources.mg.okrs.mod1_label: Add a grade derived field on metrics
resources.mg.okrs.mod1_body: Compute a 0.0 to 1.0 score alongside the tier so reports can show both. The logic is a linear interpolation between the content and optimized thresholds.
resources.mg.okrs.mod2_label: Label goal as 'Objective' in the UI
resources.mg.okrs.mod2_body: Add an OKR-mode toggle in your theme or locale file so strings like strategic goal and metric weighted render as 'Objective' and 'Key Result'. No schema changes, only copy.
resources.mg.okrs.mod3_body: For each metric in the stack, render Move {metric_name} from {baseline} to {optimized_threshold} as an auto-computed Key Result label. Helpful when exporting to slides.
resources.mg.okrs.mod4_body: Add a an OKR export handler that serializes the current cycle into the shape your legacy OKR tool consumed. Makes the concurrent-run phase cheap to operate.
resources.mg.okrs.mod5_body: If your team wants to stay in OKR vocabulary forever, leave tiers disabled in contributor components. The data still records them for leadership reporting, you just never display them to the people doing the work.
resources.mg.okrs.discover_heading: Let your team discover what fits
resources.mg.okrs.discover_body: Run the cycle. Score it twice. Ask which conversation was more useful. Whatever they tell you is the right answer, we are not the authority here.
resources.mg.okrs.cta_body: Spin up the demo, mirror your current quarter, and see what your OKR dashboard and Primer's tier view disagree about.

resources.mg.kpis.title: Running your KPI dashboard alongside Primer
resources.mg.kpis.subtitle: Your KPI dashboard is reporting. Primer is committing.
resources.mg.kpis.summary: Keep the dashboard you already trust. Add weights, tiered thresholds, and a commitment workflow on top, without losing a single number.
resources.mg.kpis.stance: KPI dashboards are the most common starting point in operationally-run companies, and for good reason: they answer the question 'are we on track right now?' with a single glance. This guide does not ask you to throw yours away. It shows how to take the KPIs you already watch and let Primer add the three things dashboards never quite gave you, explicit weight, five-tier rubric, and a target-setting negotiation that ends in a commitment.
resources.mg.kpis.concepts_intro: The vocabulary is almost unchanged. KPIs become metrics; targets become thresholds; the review cadence becomes per metric cadence.
resources.mg.kpis.row1_note: The name, unit, and owner map one-to-one.
resources.mg.kpis.row2_note: Flat target becomes the effective or optimized threshold; you add the three lower bands.
resources.mg.kpis.row3_dst: per metric cadence (weekly, monthly, quarterly, or annual)
resources.mg.kpis.row3_note: Per-metric, not per-dashboard, fast and slow KPIs can share a stack.
resources.mg.kpis.row4_dst: alarm / concern / content + effective / optimized tier
resources.mg.kpis.row4_note: Your three flags become five named bands; the top two reward performance above the target, not just meeting it.
resources.mg.kpis.row5_note: Origin tells you *why* the KPI exists (regulatory, board, assigned, self-defined).
resources.mg.kpis.concurrent_body: Do not touch your existing dashboard. Mirror the same KPIs into Primer as metrics, set tiered thresholds, and commit them through the resolution workflow. For one quarter, let your existing dashboard and Primer both report on the same numbers. Compare what each view surfaces to leadership.
resources.mg.kpis.combine_body: If the Primer view is clearer, retire the standalone dashboard and fold every KPI into the stack. The underlying data pipeline stays the same, only the presentation layer changes. Your BI queries continue to work; Primer is a consumer of the same warehouse.
resources.mg.kpis.ab_body: Run your Monday operations meeting with the old dashboard one week and the Primer tier view the next. Ask which meeting was shorter, clearer, and produced faster decisions. The answer tends to be the same after two or three cycles.
resources.mg.kpis.additions_heading: What Primer contributes beyond a standard KPI dashboard
resources.mg.kpis.additions_intro: Dashboards were designed to report. Primer is designed to commit. Here is what that means in practice.
resources.mg.kpis.addition1_body: Most KPI dashboards show every metric at equal visual weight. Primer makes priorities a declared number, the KPI that matters most is the one with the largest weight value, and the composite score reflects that.
resources.mg.kpis.addition2_label: Five tiers instead of red / yellow / green
resources.mg.kpis.addition2_body: Dashboards collapse every state into three buckets. Primer's alarm / concern / content / effective / optimized scale separates 'meeting target' from 'exceeding it', and rewards the latter.
resources.mg.kpis.addition3_body: per metric cadence from weekly to annual lets a weekly uptime KPI coexist with an annual customer-NPS metric on the same stack. Dashboards almost always impose a single refresh cycle.
resources.mg.kpis.addition4_body: the resolution workflow (draft, aligned, leader accepted, superior accepted, committed) turns target-setting from a manager's decision into a two-party negotiation. You get the same number at the end, but with buy-in and an audit trail.
resources.mg.kpis.addition5_label: lead or lag typing, leading vs lagging vs health
resources.mg.kpis.addition5_body: A KPI dashboard treats every number the same. Primer marks each metric as leading, lagging, or health, so leadership can read which way the business is moving, not only where it is.
resources.mg.kpis.modifications_heading: Customization suggestions, code you may want to modify
resources.mg.kpis.modifications_intro: The source is yours. These are the changes KPI-first teams most often make to make Primer feel like the dashboard they already had, plus the new bits.
resources.mg.kpis.mod1_label: Wire your warehouse to the current metric value
resources.mg.kpis.mod1_body: Add a scheduled ingest that reads from your BI warehouse and updates the current value on each metric.
resources.mg.kpis.mod2_label: Dashboard-style layout on the metrics page
resources.mg.kpis.mod2_body: Add a grid variant on the metric stack component that renders every metric as a tile rather than a row. Your Monday meeting keeps its dashboard muscle memory.
resources.mg.kpis.mod3_body: Inline a small time-series chart next to each metric's current tier by reading metric history.
resources.mg.kpis.mod4_body: If leadership will not let go of three-color flags, add a mapping: alarm + concern → red, content → yellow, effective + optimized → green. Keep the five-tier data underneath untouched.
resources.mg.kpis.mod5_body: Extend the alerts module to post to Slack or PagerDuty whenever a committed metric drops a tier. Matches how most teams already wire their existing dashboard.
resources.mg.kpis.discover_heading: Let your team discover what fits
resources.mg.kpis.discover_body: Run one quarter with the old dashboard and Primer both live. Ask which one leadership opens on Monday morning after two cycles. That answer is the one that counts.
resources.mg.kpis.cta_heading: Try it on one KPI first
resources.mg.kpis.cta_body: Pick one metric your team already watches. Put it into Primer with five tiers and a weight. See if leadership reads it faster.

resources.mg.bsc.title: Running the Balanced Scorecard alongside Primer
resources.mg.bsc.subtitle: Kaplan and Norton gave you perspectives and weighted measures. Primer gives you a commitment workflow on top.
resources.mg.bsc.summary: The Balanced Scorecard is the easiest framework to run inside Primer, the model already assumes weighted measures across perspectives.
resources.mg.bsc.stance: The Balanced Scorecard is a remarkable piece of engineering. Financial, Customer, Process, Learning & Growth, the four-perspective discipline has kept a generation of leadership teams honest about trade-offs between short-term and long-term, internal and external. This guide takes the BSC you already run and treats it as the first-class citizen it is: we translate perspectives to a label, targets to thresholds, and leave the rest of your practice alone.
resources.mg.bsc.concepts_heading: Translating Balanced Scorecard concepts into Primer
resources.mg.bsc.concepts_intro: BSC is arguably the closest match to Primer's native model. Perspectives become a grouping label; measures become metrics; targets become tier thresholds.
resources.mg.bsc.row1_note: One objective per perspective is the usual BSC shape.
resources.mg.bsc.row2_dst: goal label or tag + grouping in the UI
resources.mg.bsc.row2_note: Add a perspective column on goal or reuse the existing tags system.
resources.mg.bsc.row3_note: The BSC measure's unit, owner, and source map one-to-one.
resources.mg.bsc.row4_note: BSC minimum becomes the content tier; you fill in the two middle bands.
resources.mg.bsc.row5_dst: child goal linked as a supporting dependency
resources.mg.bsc.row5_note: The initiative-to-measure link is a first-class relationship, not a comment field.
resources.mg.bsc.concurrent_heading: Run Primer concurrently for a cycle
resources.mg.bsc.concurrent_body: Keep your existing BSC template. For one quarter, mirror the four perspectives as tagged goals in Primer and load the measures as metrics. Score both. BSC teams almost always find the perspectives survive intact; what changes is how targets are negotiated and locked.
resources.mg.bsc.combine_body: Fold the BSC directly into Primer's stack. The four perspectives become a tag on each strategic goal; every measure becomes a weighted metric. You do not lose anything, you gain a commitment ceremony and a five-tier rubric.
resources.mg.bsc.ab_body: Pick the perspective where leadership most often argues about what 'good' means, usually Process or Learning & Growth, and run it inside Primer for one cycle while the other three stay in your existing BSC. Compare how the quarter-end conversation goes.
resources.mg.bsc.additions_heading: What Primer contributes beyond the standard BSC model
resources.mg.bsc.additions_intro: BSC already believes in weights and perspectives. Primer adds the ceremony around committing to them.
resources.mg.bsc.addition1_body: the cycle snapshot lock freezes a quarter's targets once committed. BSC practice often relies on convention to prevent mid-quarter target changes; Primer enforces it in the model.
resources.mg.bsc.addition2_label: Five-tier rubric, not a binary pass/fail
resources.mg.bsc.addition2_body: Instead of a single target with a 'met / missed' verdict, every measure rolls up to one of five named bands. Leadership reads health at a glance.
resources.mg.bsc.addition3_body: A weekly Process measure and an annual Customer measure can live on the same scorecard. BSC tends to impose quarterly uniformity.
resources.mg.bsc.addition4_body: Draft → aligned → leader_accepted → superior_accepted → committed is the same negotiation a strong BSC practice runs informally. Primer makes it a state machine and keeps the history.
resources.mg.bsc.addition5_label: Strategic initiative as a typed relationship
resources.mg.bsc.addition5_body: typed goal dependencies with supports makes 'this initiative moves that measure' a queryable link, not a footnote on a PowerPoint slide.
resources.mg.bsc.modifications_heading: Customization suggestions, code you may want to modify
resources.mg.bsc.modifications_intro: These are the most common changes BSC teams make so that Primer renders like the scorecard they already know.
resources.mg.bsc.mod1_label: Add a perspective field on goal
resources.mg.bsc.mod1_body: Introduce a typed perspective label on strategic goals and surface it in the filter sidebar.
resources.mg.bsc.mod2_body: Build a the BSC view that lays out the four perspectives in a 2×2 grid. Reuses the existing metric card for each cell.
resources.mg.bsc.mod3_body: Render the goal dependency graph as a top-down causal map. The typed goal dependencies table already has what you need, the work is in the rendering.
resources.mg.bsc.mod4_body: Extend the composite score calculation so each perspective contributes a declared share. Default is 25/25/25/25; most BSC teams override within the first month.
resources.mg.bsc.mod5_body: Add a PDF route that generates the classic BSC scorecard layout from the current snapshot. Board members who expect the Kaplan & Norton layout keep getting it.
resources.mg.bsc.discover_heading: Let your team discover what fits
resources.mg.bsc.discover_body: BSC practitioners tend to be among the fastest to adopt Primer because so much of the model is already familiar. Run a cycle and see whether the snapshot lock and five-tier rubric feel like a natural extension of what you already do.
resources.mg.bsc.cta_body: Pick Financial or Process, whichever has the tightest numbers, and mirror it into Primer for one quarter.

resources.mg.four_dx.title: Running 4DX alongside Primer
resources.mg.four_dx.subtitle: WIGs, lead measures, lag measures, compelling scoreboard, cadence of accountability, already natively modeled.
resources.mg.four_dx.summary: If you run the 4 Disciplines of Execution, Primer may be the cleanest fit of any framework in the catalog.
resources.mg.four_dx.stance: 4DX is a disciplined system. The distinction between lead and lag measures, the insistence on a compelling scoreboard, and the weekly cadence of accountability are exactly the behaviors we have watched Primer users reinvent for themselves. This guide is not selling you on 4DX; you already bought in. It is showing how lead or lag typing, per metric cadence, and the metric stack line up with WIGs, lead / lag measures, and the scoreboard, so you can run 4DX with tooling that understands the discipline.
resources.mg.four_dx.concepts_intro: Every discipline has a one-to-one Primer equivalent, no forcing required.
resources.mg.four_dx.row1_note: WIG discipline (focus on the wildly important) maps to Primer's strategic typing.
resources.mg.four_dx.row2_note: The outcome you want, what the quarter is judged on.
resources.mg.four_dx.row3_note: The behavior you can influence today that moves the lag measure later.
resources.mg.four_dx.row4_dst: tier rollup view of the metric stack
resources.mg.four_dx.row4_note: Five colored bands you can read from across the room, the point of a compelling scoreboard.
resources.mg.four_dx.row5_dst: per metric cadence on each metric + the weekly the resolution workflow review
resources.mg.four_dx.row5_note: Weekly WIG sessions align with Primer's commitment workflow.
resources.mg.four_dx.concurrent_body: Keep your WIG session and your paper scoreboard. Mirror the lead and lag measures into Primer and let both systems compute their scoreboards. Most 4DX teams see identical conclusions within a week, which is the point.
resources.mg.four_dx.combine_body: Retire the paper scoreboard. WIGs become strategic goals; lead and lag measures become typed metrics; the weekly cadence is already in per metric cadence. Your existing 4DX coach does not need to rewrite anything, the vocabulary is preserved.
resources.mg.four_dx.ab_body: Give one team the Primer scoreboard and the other the paper one. Run 4DX for a cycle. Ask both which scoreboard made their WIG session shorter and sharper.
resources.mg.four_dx.additions_heading: What Primer contributes beyond the standard 4DX practice
resources.mg.four_dx.additions_intro: 4DX is already disciplined. Primer adds machinery for when the discipline has to scale beyond one team.
resources.mg.four_dx.addition1_label: Typed lead / lag at the schema level
resources.mg.four_dx.addition1_body: lead or lag typing encodes the lead/lag distinction in the database. Reports can filter or group by it, no discipline drift when new measures are added.
resources.mg.four_dx.addition2_body: 4DX does not specify how to weigh WIGs against each other. Primer's metric-level weight gives you a first-class answer when leadership has to trade one WIG for another.
resources.mg.four_dx.addition3_body: Draft → aligned → committed turns a WIG target from a decision into a negotiation with a paper trail. Helpful when WIGs have to cascade across teams.
resources.mg.four_dx.addition4_body: The the audit log log captures every change to a measure's value or threshold. The classic 4DX failure mode. 'did we quietly move the line?', becomes answerable with a query.
resources.mg.four_dx.addition5_body: If your WIG relies on another team's lead measure, typed goal dependencies with blocks or informs makes that link explicit and visible in both scoreboards.
resources.mg.four_dx.modifications_headi: Customization suggestions, code you may want to modify
resources.mg.four_dx.modifications_intro: 4DX shops are fiercely opinionated about the visual scoreboard. Here is where customers most often customize to match their existing language.
resources.mg.four_dx.mod1_label: Label lead or lag typing as 'Lead' / 'Lag' in the UI
resources.mg.four_dx.mod1_body: Trivial locale change, update the strings for indicator.leading and indicator.lagging to 'Lead measure' and 'Lag measure' so 4DX veterans feel at home immediately.
resources.mg.four_dx.mod2_body: Add a a compelling scoreboard page route that renders the current tier state of every lead and lag measure in a big, high-contrast format designed to be projected in a war room.
resources.mg.four_dx.mod3_body: Add a small form component where each participant commits to one action for the upcoming week. Store it as a goal_adjustment note so the accountability thread is permanent.
resources.mg.four_dx.mod4_body: A dedicated chart type where the lead measure's week-over-week progress is shown above its lag measure's monthly roll. About 50 lines with your existing chart library.
resources.mg.four_dx.mod5_body: Render the cascaded origin tree as a top-down WIG map so the whole organization sees how their WIG supports the WIG above.
resources.mg.four_dx.discover_heading: Let your team discover what fits
resources.mg.four_dx.discover_body: 4DX teams usually need fewer cycles than any other group to decide. The discipline is the same; only the tooling changes.
resources.mg.four_dx.cta_heading: Ready to project it on the wall?
resources.mg.four_dx.cta_body: Spin up the demo, mirror your current WIGs, and see what the compelling scoreboard looks like rendered from real data.

resources.mg.eos.title: Running EOS / Traction alongside Primer
resources.mg.eos.subtitle: Rocks, Scorecard, and the Level 10 meeting. Primer runs the data; you keep the rhythm.
resources.mg.eos.summary: EOS (the Entrepreneurial Operating System, described in Gino Wickman's Traction) is built around quarterly Rocks, a weekly Scorecard, and a strict meeting cadence. Primer fits the first two cleanly; the meeting stays yours.
resources.mg.eos.stance: EOS earned its place in thousands of founder-led companies the hard way: it works because the meeting rhythm is sacred and the Scorecard is weekly. Primer is not trying to replace the V/TO, the People Analyzer, or the Level 10 meeting, those are your practice and they stay intact. What this guide shows is how Rocks and the Scorecard map into Primer's goal and metric model so the data side of EOS gets a proper home while the meeting rhythm keeps running the same way it does today.
resources.mg.eos.concepts_intro: Every EOS vocabulary word Primer can model, it models directly. The two it cannot, the V/TO and the Level 10 meeting, are flagged honestly.
resources.mg.eos.row1_note: Due date at quarter end, with origin tracked as self defined or co authored.
resources.mg.eos.row2_note: Scorecard metric → metric with weekly per metric cadence. Red/green flags → tiers.
resources.mg.eos.row3_dst: metric with tagged as leading or 'lagging'
resources.mg.eos.row3_note: EOS prefers lead measures; Primer encodes the preference in schema.
resources.mg.eos.row4_note: 'Who' stays with each row, matching EOS accountability discipline.
resources.mg.eos.row5_note: Primer does not replace your meeting artifacts. Keep your V/TO document and your Level 10 agenda untouched.
resources.mg.eos.concurrent_body: Keep your Scorecard spreadsheet and your Rock tracker. For one quarter, let Primer also carry the same Rocks and Scorecard metrics. Score both. Most EOS integrators report that the spreadsheet fades after two quarters because the Primer view is cleaner and leads the Level 10 meeting better, but this is your call, not ours.
resources.mg.eos.combine_body: Load every Rock as an operational goal and every Scorecard row as a weekly metric. Your Level 10 meeting now opens Primer instead of a spreadsheet. The V/TO stays in whatever tool you already keep it in, we do not encroach.
resources.mg.eos.ab_body: Pick one department, the one most invested in lead measures, and run their weekly Scorecard in Primer while the rest of the company stays on the spreadsheet. Compare the Level 10 reviews after a quarter.
resources.mg.eos.additions_heading: What Primer contributes beyond the standard EOS model
resources.mg.eos.additions_intro: EOS is intentionally spreadsheet-friendly. Primer is where you gain when the company outgrows the spreadsheet.
resources.mg.eos.addition1_body: EOS is typically red/green on the Scorecard. Primer's five-tier rubric separates 'we just met the number' from 'we crushed it' so the Level 10 conversation has more resolution.
resources.mg.eos.addition2_body: EOS treats all Rocks as equal weight by convention. Primer's explicit weight lets the leadership team say, in writing, which Rock matters most this quarter.
resources.mg.eos.addition3_body: cascaded origin plus typed goal dependencies turn 'this Rock depends on another team finishing their Rock' from a meeting note into a queryable relationship.
resources.mg.eos.addition4_body: the resolution workflow turns Rock target-setting into a draft → aligned → committed negotiation with a history. Useful for distributed leadership teams who cannot always be in the same room.
resources.mg.eos.addition5_body: Once the quarter's Rocks are committed and the cycle snapshot lock is set, targets cannot be quietly moved. Rock drift is one of the most common EOS failure modes; Primer solves it at the schema layer.
resources.mg.eos.modifications_heading: Customization suggestions, code you may want to modify
resources.mg.eos.modifications_intro: EOS practitioners are protective of the vocabulary, rightly. Here are the customizations customers most often make so Primer sounds like EOS in their instance.
resources.mg.eos.mod1_label: Label operational goals as 'Rocks' in the UI
resources.mg.eos.mod1_body: A trivial copy change. The EOS vocabulary shows up everywhere a goal type is displayed.
resources.mg.eos.mod2_body: A single table with one row per metric and one column per week of the quarter. Classic EOS Scorecard shape, rendered from the metric history table.
resources.mg.eos.mod3_body: Add a a Level 10 meeting page route that loads the current Scorecard at the top and leaves room for Issues, To-Dos, and Headlines underneath. Your facilitator gets a ready-made agenda.
resources.mg.eos.mod4_body: Add a simple Issues table that can reference org_a goal. When a Level 10 surfaces an Issue about a Rock, it persists against that Rock permanently.
resources.mg.eos.mod5_body: Export every Rock + its Scorecard metrics as a single PDF your EOS Implementer can walk the leadership team through. Mirrors the spreadsheet hand-off they already expect.
resources.mg.eos.discover_heading: Let your team discover what fits
resources.mg.eos.discover_body: The meeting rhythm is yours to keep, always. All Primer can offer is a better data layer underneath it. Run a quarter and see whether the Level 10 meeting feels tighter.

resources.mg.eos.cta_body: Load one Rock and its Scorecard metric into Primer. Use it in the next Level 10. Decide from there.

resources.mg.hoshin.title: Running Hoshin Kanri alongside Primer
resources.mg.hoshin.subtitle: Catchball, the X-matrix, and annual breakthroughs, encoded in Primer's schema, not a spreadsheet.
resources.mg.hoshin.summary: Hoshin Kanri's catchball negotiation maps exactly to Primer's the resolution workflow workflow. The X-matrix cascade maps to cascaded goals with source tracking.
resources.mg.hoshin.stance: Hoshin Kanri came out of Toyota, earned its place through decades of lean practice, and is arguably the most disciplined policy-deployment system in modern management. If your team runs Hoshin, you have invested real time in the catchball ritual and the X-matrix discipline, this guide is not asking you to throw either away. It shows how Primer's schema already carries the concepts you use, so the X-matrix stops living in a spreadsheet and starts living in a model designed for it.
resources.mg.hoshin.concepts_heading: Translating Hoshin Kanri concepts into Primer
resources.mg.hoshin.concepts_intro: The lean community will recognize every row, this is one of the smoothest conceptual fits of any framework in the catalog.
resources.mg.hoshin.row1_note: Typically 3–5 per organization, annual. Primer's strategic typing fits exactly.
resources.mg.hoshin.row2_dst: child goal with per metric cadence = 'annual'
resources.mg.hoshin.row2_note: Rolls up to a breakthrough via typed goal dependencies.
resources.mg.hoshin.row3_dst: the resolution workflow (draft, aligned, leader accepted, superior accepted, committed)
resources.mg.hoshin.row3_note: The single closest one-to-one match between any external framework and Primer's internals.
resources.mg.hoshin.row4_dst: cascaded goal plus typed dependency links
resources.mg.hoshin.row4_note: The four quadrants of the X-matrix become typed dependency links.
resources.mg.hoshin.row5_dst: per metric cadence + snapshot locking on the cycle
resources.mg.hoshin.row5_note: Locked snapshots match the lean principle of freezing the plan to expose variance.
resources.mg.hoshin.concurrent_body: Keep your X-matrix spreadsheet. For one Hoshin cycle, mirror the breakthrough objectives and annual improvement priorities into Primer as goals, and let Primer record each catchball round as a the resolution workflow transition. Compare the catchball history at year-end: the spreadsheet version almost always loses some rounds; Primer preserves all of them.
resources.mg.hoshin.combine_body: Replace the spreadsheet X-matrix with Primer's goal dependency view. Every breakthrough, every annual priority, and every cascaded subordinate goal lives in one queryable place, and the catchball negotiation is captured as first-class data instead of Excel comments.
resources.mg.hoshin.ab_body: Pick the breakthrough whose catchball usually drags the longest and run it through Primer's resolution workflow while the others stay in the spreadsheet. Ask the team which felt faster.
resources.mg.hoshin.additions_heading: What Primer contributes beyond the standard Hoshin practice
resources.mg.hoshin.additions_intro: Hoshin's vocabulary is already rich. Primer's value is in the schema underneath, so the vocabulary stops being enforced by discipline alone.
resources.mg.hoshin.addition1_body: Typed relationships (blocks, informs, supports) encode the four X matrix quadrants. Your strategy map is a query, not a slide.
resources.mg.hoshin.addition2_body: Every resolution transition is logged in the audit log with who, when, and why. The round-by-round catchball conversation becomes an audit trail leadership can walk years later.
resources.mg.hoshin.addition3_body: Hoshin typically uses red/yellow/green at review time. Primer's alarm / concern / content / effective / optimized gives reviewers more resolution without adding meetings.
resources.mg.hoshin.addition4_body: Once the cycle is committed, targets freeze. Lean teams already believe in this discipline; Primer enforces it in the schema rather than relying on culture.
resources.mg.hoshin.addition5_body: Breakthroughs can be annual while the cascaded subordinate goals are quarterly. Primer carries both on the same graph without confusing either. Most X-matrix spreadsheets cannot.
resources.mg.hoshin.modifications_headin: Customization suggestions, code you may want to modify
resources.mg.hoshin.modifications_intro: Hoshin shops tend to be deeply invested in the X-matrix layout. Here are the customizations that make Primer feel like a native Hoshin tool.
resources.mg.hoshin.mod1_body: Build an the X matrix view that reads the four categories of typed goal dependencies and renders them as the classic four-quadrant X. Heavy UI work, but the payoff is immediate recognition.
resources.mg.hoshin.mod2_body: Add a derived count on each goal showing how many draft → aligned cycles it took to reach commitment. Surface it in the review view, it becomes a health metric of the practice itself.
resources.mg.hoshin.mod3_body: A PDF route that renders the year-end catchball history and tier scoring in the standard Hoshin report shape lean auditors expect.
resources.mg.hoshin.mod4_body: If your team uses the Japanese Hoshin vocabulary, update the ja.json locale for the resolution workflow state names. Purely a copy change.
resources.mg.hoshin.mod5_body: A dedicated review route that groups goals by their cascaded ancestor so the Hoshin facilitator can walk top-down through every breakthrough in a single session.
resources.mg.hoshin.discover_heading: Let your team discover what fits
resources.mg.hoshin.discover_body: Hoshin practitioners usually recognize Primer as a native home for the practice within an hour. Run one cycle and see whether the catchball history alone justifies the switch.
resources.mg.hoshin.cta_body: Pick a breakthrough whose catchball always drags. Put it into Primer's resolution workflow and see how it lands.

resources.mg.mbo.title: Running Management by Objectives alongside Primer
resources.mg.mbo.subtitle: Drucker's original model still works. Primer adds an operational layer on top without disturbing the practice.
resources.mg.mbo.summary: MBO is the ancestor of every modern goal framework. Primer's goal maps directly; the added value is everything around it, metrics, tiers, cadence, dependency.
resources.mg.mbo.stance: Peter Drucker introduced MBO in 1954 and it is still running productively in traditional enterprises today. It works because it gets the fundamental thing right: align the individual's objectives to the organization's objectives and review them on a cadence. This guide does not ask you to replace any of that. It walks through how the objectives you already set each cycle map cleanly onto Primer's goal model, and what optional layers sit on top if you ever want them.
resources.mg.mbo.concepts_intro: MBO is the closest one-to-one match of any framework in the catalog. Primer's goal was arguably designed with MBO in mind.
resources.mg.mbo.row2_note: Department objectives flow down; use cascaded origin to preserve the link.
resources.mg.mbo.row3_note: Drucker's 'management by self-control' maps to each person owning their own objective row.
resources.mg.mbo.row4_note: MBO was built for long review cycles; Primer respects that cadence.
resources.mg.mbo.row5_dst: the resolution workflow scoring + five-tier rubric
resources.mg.mbo.row5_note: Optional. MBO's traditional met/partially-met/missed grading works too.
resources.mg.mbo.concurrent_body: Nothing about your review process changes. For one cycle, enter the same objectives your people already own into Primer in parallel with your HR review tool. Use Primer as a read-only layer for leadership visibility; use your HR tool as the performance-review system of record.
resources.mg.mbo.combine_body: Load every objective, organizational, departmental, individual, as an goal with the appropriate type and assignee. Optionally add metrics for the numeric objectives. Your existing annual review discipline stays intact; Primer becomes the place the objectives live and roll up between reviews.
resources.mg.mbo.ab_body: Pick a department that feels comfortable with instrumentation and run its MBO cycle in Primer while the rest of the company stays in whatever tool you use today. Compare end-of-cycle conversations.
resources.mg.mbo.additions_heading: What Primer contributes beyond the standard MBO model
resources.mg.mbo.additions_intro: MBO travels very light by design. Primer's additions are all optional and can be ignored without breaking the practice.
resources.mg.mbo.addition1_body: MBO reviews objectives once or twice a year. Primer's metric stack runs continuously underneath, so if you ever want between-review visibility you have it, without changing the review cadence itself.
resources.mg.mbo.addition2_body: MBO traditionally grades objectives met / partially met / missed. Primer's five tiers are available if leadership wants more resolution, but they are opt-in; the two-value grade works fine.
resources.mg.mbo.addition3_body: Drucker's cascade from corporate to department to individual is encoded in typed goal dependencies as typed relationships. A single query shows every individual objective that traces up to a given corporate objective.
resources.mg.mbo.addition4_body: the audit log logs every change to an objective's wording, owner, or target. Helpful when HR is asked, years later, to show how an individual's objective was set.
resources.mg.mbo.addition5_body: goal type: strategic | operational | developmental | compliance lets classic MBO objectives live on the same list as compliance objectives (SOC2, ISO) without confusing the two.
resources.mg.mbo.modifications_heading: Customization suggestions, code you may want to modify
resources.mg.mbo.modifications_intro: MBO is stable and disciplined by design. These customizations are usually about presentation and HR-tool integration rather than new features.
resources.mg.mbo.mod1_body: Add an export endpoint that posts the current cycle's objectives into your HR review system at the cadence HR expects.
resources.mg.mbo.mod2_body: Generate a per-person PDF at review time with every objective, the tier or grade, and any notes from the audit log. Mirrors the review packet HR already prepares.
resources.mg.mbo.mod3_body: Update the locale to show alarm/concern as 'Missed', content as 'Partially met', and effective/optimized as 'Met / Exceeded'. Keeps HR language unchanged.
resources.mg.mbo.mod4_body: A simple a 'my objectives' page route where each person sees only their assigned objectives. Fits Drucker's principle of management by self-control.
resources.mg.mbo.mod5_body: A page that groups objectives by department and shows aggregate cadence compliance. Useful for senior leadership reviews in traditional org structures.
resources.mg.mbo.discover_heading: Let your team discover what fits
resources.mg.mbo.discover_body: MBO shops are usually the slowest to change tools because MBO works, and good for them. Run Primer for one review cycle alongside your existing practice and decide only if the visibility is worth it.
resources.mg.mbo.cta_heading: Try it for one review cycle
resources.mg.mbo.cta_body: Load this year's objectives into Primer and use it as a read-only view through the cycle. Revisit at review time.

resources.mg.v2mom.title: Running V2MOM alongside Primer
resources.mg.v2mom.subtitle: Vision, Values, Methods, Obstacles, Measures, the Salesforce model keeps its narrative; Primer runs the measures.
resources.mg.v2mom.summary: Marc Benioff's V2MOM is held together by narrative. Primer leaves the narrative where it is and takes ownership of the measures layer.
resources.mg.v2mom.stance: V2MOM works because each company builds its own Vision and Values statement and then cascades through Methods, Obstacles, and Measures. The narrative discipline is the whole point, we are not going to ask you to trade that for a table. This guide is about giving the Measures row of every V2MOM a proper data home while leaving the Vision, Values, Methods, and Obstacles exactly where they live today.
resources.mg.v2mom.concepts_intro: Only Measures map to Primer's core model. The other four V-rows stay in whatever narrative document you already use.
resources.mg.v2mom.row1_dst: (out of scope, stays in your V2MOM document)
resources.mg.v2mom.row1_note: Vision is narrative, not data. Primer does not try to model it.
resources.mg.v2mom.row2_note: Same, values live in your V2MOM doc.
resources.mg.v2mom.row3_note: Each Method is an operational goal the team owns.
resources.mg.v2mom.row4_note: Obstacles become blocking dependencies between goals, made queryable and visible.
resources.mg.v2mom.row5_note: Five-tier thresholds encode the V2MOM target and the guardrails around it.
resources.mg.v2mom.concurrent_body: Keep your V2MOM document verbatim. Copy the Measures row into Primer as metrics and the Methods row as operational goals. Let Primer compute health and leave the narrative rows where they are.
resources.mg.v2mom.combine_body: Store a link to the V2MOM document on each associated strategic goal. Primer then becomes the place you click into when a leader asks 'how are we tracking against the Measures we wrote this year?'
resources.mg.v2mom.ab_body: Pick the V2MOM whose Measures row is hardest to track in the existing document. Mirror it into Primer and see whether the quarterly reviews feel tighter.
resources.mg.v2mom.additions_heading: What Primer contributes beyond the standard V2MOM practice
resources.mg.v2mom.additions_intro: V2MOM is narrative-first by design. Primer adds the data-first layer that narrative alone cannot compute.
resources.mg.v2mom.addition1_body: Obstacles become blocks links in the goal graph, not bullet points. If an Obstacle changes shape, the dependency graph reflects it immediately.
resources.mg.v2mom.addition2_body: Each Measure gets five named bands, not just a single number. Leadership can see trajectory, not only current state.
resources.mg.v2mom.addition3_body: Once the annual V2MOM is committed, Measures freeze. No quiet mid-year target revision without a recorded reason.
resources.mg.v2mom.addition4_body: Departmental V2MOMs link to the corporate V2MOM through cascaded goals. A data view complements the narrative view.
resources.mg.v2mom.addition5_body: V2MOMs are typically annual. If some Measures need a weekly pulse, per metric cadence on the individual metric carries that without changing the V2MOM itself.
resources.mg.v2mom.modifications_heading: Customization suggestions, code you may want to modify
resources.mg.v2mom.modifications_intro: V2MOM-heavy teams usually care most about keeping the narrative document central. Customizations tend to be about linking back, not replacing.
resources.mg.v2mom.mod1_body: Add a simple V2MOM link column on strategic goals. The page renders it as a prominent hyperlink back to the narrative document.
resources.mg.v2mom.mod2_label: Vision / Values banner on the dashboard
resources.mg.v2mom.mod2_body: A small, persistent banner on top of the metric view showing the corporate Vision and Values statements. Pure copy change in your theme.
resources.mg.v2mom.mod3_body: A dedicated page listing every blocks dependency across the organization, the V2MOM Obstacles row turned into a live operational queue.
resources.mg.v2mom.mod4_body: A PDF route that interleaves the narrative sections with the Primer-computed Measures roll-up. Ready to hand to the board.
resources.mg.v2mom.mod5_body: A form where the owner of each Method adds a one-paragraph narrative update per quarter, stored alongside the goal for end-of-year review.
resources.mg.v2mom.discover_heading: Let your team discover what fits
resources.mg.v2mom.discover_body: V2MOM practitioners usually want the narrative left alone. Use Primer as the data layer, not the voice, and let the team decide how much of each they want.
resources.mg.v2mom.cta_heading: Try it on this year's Measures row
resources.mg.v2mom.cta_body: Load the Measures row of your current corporate V2MOM into Primer. The narrative stays wherever you keep it today.

resources.mg.kras.title: Running KRAs alongside Primer
resources.mg.kras.subtitle: Key Result Areas stay intact. Primer adds measurement and cadence on top of each area.
resources.mg.kras.summary: Key Result Areas are widely used across South Asia and traditional manufacturing enterprises. Primer models them directly and adds a continuous metric layer.
resources.mg.kras.stance: KRAs (Key Result Areas) are especially common in South Asian enterprises and traditional manufacturing organizations. They work because they define the zones of responsibility, the areas a role is judged on, rather than the numbers themselves. This guide maps each KRA to a Primer goal and shows how metrics can live underneath each area without disturbing the KRA structure leadership already knows.
resources.mg.kras.concepts_intro: The KRA structure maps cleanly onto Primer's goal model, a KRA is essentially an operational goal with measurable metrics underneath.
resources.mg.kras.row1_note: One goal per KRA, owned by the role that role-carries it.
resources.mg.kras.row2_dst: metric weight (summing to 100 across the role's KRAs)
resources.mg.kras.row2_note: Primer's weight primitive matches the KRA-weighting convention exactly.
resources.mg.kras.row3_dst: metric in the quintile stack, parented to the KRA goal
resources.mg.kras.row3_note: Each KPI becomes a tiered metric with explicit thresholds.
resources.mg.kras.row4_dst: the resolution workflow plus composite score
resources.mg.kras.row4_note: Traditional 1–5 appraisal ratings map to the five-tier rubric.
resources.mg.kras.row5_note: Role remains the unit of accountability. Primer preserves it.
resources.mg.kras.concurrent_body: For one appraisal cycle, mirror each role's KRAs and KPIs into Primer and score both sides at cycle-end. Compare the conversations the HR appraisal produced against the conversation Primer's tier view produced.
resources.mg.kras.combine_body: Load every role's KRAs as operational goals and every KPI as a tiered metric. The appraisal rating remains whatever HR wants it to be, but the data feeding it is now continuous rather than looked up at the last minute.
resources.mg.kras.ab_body: Pick the department whose KRAs are tightest against measurable KPIs, usually Sales or Operations, and run them in Primer for one cycle.
resources.mg.kras.additions_heading: What Primer contributes beyond the standard KRA model
resources.mg.kras.additions_intro: KRAs already believe in weighted areas. Primer adds the operational layer that annual appraisal cycles almost always leave blank between reviews.
resources.mg.kras.addition1_body: KRAs are typically evaluated once a year. Primer's metric stack reads current values continuously, so the year-end appraisal is never a surprise.
resources.mg.kras.addition2_body: Appraisals collapse KPIs into 1–5 ratings. Primer keeps the underlying data in five named bands that map directly to those ratings when it is time to score.
resources.mg.kras.addition3_body: KRA spreadsheets often drift where weights do not sum to 100. Primer enforces the sum in the data model, leadership literally cannot ship a mis-weighted stack.
resources.mg.kras.addition4_body: Every adjustment to a rating or threshold is logged. When HR is asked two years later why a rating was set the way it was, the answer exists.
resources.mg.kras.addition5_body: Aggregate performance across a role or department falls out of the schema for free, no separate reporting tool required.
resources.mg.kras.modifications_heading: Customization suggestions, code you may want to modify
resources.mg.kras.modifications_intro: KRA-heavy organizations tend to have strong HR-tool integration requirements. These customizations are the most common.
resources.mg.kras.mod1_body: Add an endpoint that exports each role's KRA scores into the format your HR system expects at appraisal time, usually a CSV or SAP HR feed.
resources.mg.kras.mod2_body: Trivial locale change, alarm=1, concern=2, content=3, effective=4, optimized=5. Matches the traditional KRA rating scale exactly.
resources.mg.kras.mod3_body: A a role page route that renders every KRA and KPI for a given role as a single-page appraisal packet.
resources.mg.kras.mod4_body: Configure Primer's snapshot lock to auto-run at the start of each appraisal window. Ratings then cannot drift during the appraisal itself.
resources.mg.kras.mod5_body: If your organization runs appraisals in multiple regional languages, the existing i18n system already supports the locale switch, you just need to translate the KRA-specific strings.
resources.mg.kras.discover_heading: Let your team discover what fits
resources.mg.kras.discover_body: KRA-run organizations tend to value stability above all else. Run Primer for one appraisal cycle and change nothing about the HR practice. See if the continuous visibility alone justifies it.
resources.mg.kras.cta_body: Pick one role, sales, operations, or a functional head, and load its KRAs into Primer for one appraisal cycle.

seo.personas.og_title: Deploy Primer for your clients. Keep it running when you leave.
seo.personas.og_description: PE operators, fractional CFOs, EOS implementers, and ops consultants buy Primer on behalf of the companies they serve.
personas.hero: Your client buys it. You set it up. It keeps running after you leave.
personas.hero_sub: Each license belongs to one legal entity at $5,000, perpetual, with full source code. You bring the framework, configure it during the engagement, and hand over something that works without you.
personas.pe.headline: One scorecard. Every portco. No new SaaS contract per company.
personas.pe.body1: You own twelve portfolio companies, and every one of them has a different performance tool. Lattice at one, 15Five at another, spreadsheets at three, and nothing at all at the rest. You've been asked to standardize, and every vendor quote comes back wanting a multi-year contract per portco plus implementation fees per portco. The math doesn't work.
personas.pe.body2: Primer is a one-time $5,000 license per legal entity. Each portco buys its own license, you deploy the same framework across all of them, and the operating team gets a standard five-tier scorecard they can actually compare across the portfolio. Total cost for twelve portcos: $60,000, one time, forever. No renewals. No seat fees as a portco grows. No consolidation headache when one gets sold.
personas.pe.body3: Every metric has an owner, a weight, and a tier. Every leader sees a composite score for their scope. Cross-functional disputes go through a formal Inquiry process that doesn't require you to fly to the portco to resolve it. When you swap a CEO, the incoming CEO sees exactly where the business stands on day one, in the same format as every other business in the fund.
personas.pe.next_step: Take 30 minutes to walk one portco team through the framework. If they don't get it, you haven't committed to anything. If they do, the portco buys the license that afternoon.
personas.cfo.headline: Stop line-iteming a performance tool. Own it and move on.
personas.cfo.body1: You walk into a 75-person company and find they're paying $24,000 a year for a performance tool that three managers actually use. You recommend cancelling it, and HR pushes back because they picked it, and now the cancellation is political. The actual problem — nobody in the company agrees on what 'good' looks like for any function — isn't being solved by the tool they're paying for.
personas.cfo.body2: Primer turns the performance-management line into a capital expense instead of an operating expense. One payment of $5,000, amortized across the useful life of the asset, hits the balance sheet the way an ERP or an accounting package does. It's the kind of purchase a CFO approves without asking for permission, because it's smaller than a laptop refresh and it replaces a recurring cost.
personas.cfo.body3: Primer also gives you a weighted composite score for every leader in the company. When you sit down with the CEO for the monthly review, you can point at a number and say 'this is where operations is, this is where sales is, this is where engineering is, and here is what each team agreed counts.' It becomes a CFO's dashboard as much as an HR tool, which is why CFOs end up being the ones who recommend it.
personas.cfo.next_step: Run the cost calculator on the current performance tool. Five-year savings are almost always six figures at 100+ headcount. The number does the selling.
personas.eos.headline: A real scorecard. Your clients will actually open it.
personas.eos.body1: You've been running EOS engagements for four years. Every client gets a scorecard spreadsheet in session two, promises to update it every Monday, and then the spreadsheet rots by session six. You walk into the Level 10 meeting to find last month's numbers, or no numbers, or numbers that only one person on the leadership team actually believes. The framework works. The tool for running it does not.
personas.eos.body2: Primer implements the scorecard the way EOS describes it: each leader owns their measurables, each measurable has a clear standard, and the whole leadership team sees the same numbers on the same cadence. The five tiers (Alarm, Concern, Content, Effective, Optimized) map onto the red/yellow/green language your clients already use, with more resolution so 'yellow' doesn't mean three different things to three different leaders.
personas.eos.body3: Because Primer ships as source code, you rebrand it once, load your own metric templates, and deploy it at every client engagement. Each client buys their own license at $5,000, one-time. When you walk out at the end of the engagement, the client owns the source and the deployment, and your work keeps running without you.
personas.eos.next_step: Load Primer on your laptop this week. Spend 20 minutes adding the measurables from your next client's V/TO and bring the running product to your next session. The conversation changes the moment they see their own numbers in the tier view.
personas.consultant.headline: Leave something running when the engagement ends.
personas.consultant.body1: You've spent fifteen years helping companies fix their operations. Every engagement follows the same arc: you diagnose the problem, build a framework, train the team, and hand over a spreadsheet or a deck that's supposed to keep it all going. Six months later, the spreadsheet is stale, the deck is in someone's archive folder, and the client calls you back to do it again.
personas.consultant.body2: Primer is the tool that breaks that cycle. Your client buys a $5,000 perpetual license for their legal entity, and you configure it during the engagement: their metrics, their weights, their tier definitions. The framework you built lives inside a running application their team opens every week instead of a file they forget exists. You rebrand it to match the client's identity or your own firm's, and the source code means you can add whatever custom views the engagement calls for.
personas.consultant.body3: When you walk out, the client owns the deployment and the source. They don't need you to keep it running, and they don't need DavidPM either. But here's what actually happens: clients who use Primer during an engagement call you back for the next engagement, not because the tool broke, but because the framework worked and they want you to extend it. That's the business model. You stop being the person who diagnoses problems and start being the person who built the system that solved them.
personas.consultant.next_step: Pick your next engagement. Load the client's top five operational metrics into Primer on your laptop. Walk into the kickoff meeting with a running product instead of a slide deck.

seo.pitch_deck.og_description: Problem, cost comparison, data sovereignty, and the close. Drop your name on slide 12 and send it.
pitch_deck.s1.title: Performance management you actually own.
pitch_deck.s1.body: A goal management system your people use every week, not a form they fill out once a year. One payment, complete source code, behind your firewall.
pitch_deck.s2.title: The system designed to improve performance actively damages it.
pitch_deck.s2.stat1: of managers are frustrated with their performance system
pitch_deck.s2.stat2: of Fortune 500 CHROs say their system inspires employees
pitch_deck.s2.stat3: spent per year on reviews at a 10,000-person company
pitch_deck.s3.title: Why the existing tools don't fix it.
pitch_deck.s3.roadmap_body: Most tools digitize the annual review. They make the form prettier. They don't change what happens the other 364 days.
pitch_deck.s3.rent_body: Charts and reports look great in a demo. But if nobody opens the tool on Monday morning, the data doesn't drive action.
pitch_deck.s3.data_body: HR gets a completed form. The employee gets a rating. Neither gets a system that tells them what to work on next.
pitch_deck.s4.title: Five tiers. Not a score. Not a rating.
pitch_deck.s4.body: Every person in the organization knows exactly where they stand. Not on a 1-to-10 scale, but in one of five meaningful states that drive different actions.
pitch_deck.s4.optimized_label: Operating at peak, ready to mentor
pitch_deck.s5.title: Metrics people actually own.
pitch_deck.s5.body: Goal management fails when metrics are imposed from the top. Primer builds ownership into the structure.
pitch_deck.s5.propose_body: Senior leadership defines the metrics that matter for each role: safety, throughput, client satisfaction, whatever drives the business.
pitch_deck.s5.approve_body: Direct managers review and adjust metrics for their team's context. No metric goes live without the manager's sign-off.
pitch_deck.s5.own_body: Every person sees exactly what they're measured on and why. No surprises at review time. The metrics are theirs.
pitch_deck.s5.weight_body: When safety is 40% and schedule is 30%, everyone knows the strategic priority. It's math, not a suggestion.
pitch_deck.s5.challenge_body: The Inquiry mechanism resolves cross-functional conflicts through structure, not politics and hallway conversations.
pitch_deck.s6.title: What your team actually sees.
pitch_deck.s6.body: Primer is designed for weekly use, not quarterly check-ins. Every role gets a view built for their decisions.
pitch_deck.s6.ic_body: See your metrics, your tier, your trend. Know exactly what to focus on this week. No ambiguity.
pitch_deck.s6.manager_body: See every direct report's tier at a glance. Spot who needs a conversation before it becomes a crisis.
pitch_deck.s6.leader_body: Composite scores across teams and departments. One view to see where the organization is strong and where it's drifting.
pitch_deck.s6.realtime_body: Tiers update as data comes in. By the time you sit down for a review, both sides already know the story.
pitch_deck.s7.title: Bring what you already run.
pitch_deck.s7.body: Primer isn't a replacement for your goals. It's the system that makes them operational. Map any framework into the five-tier model.
pitch_deck.s7.okr_body: Objectives become metric groups. Key results become weighted metrics with tier thresholds.
pitch_deck.s7.kpi_body: Each KPI maps directly to a metric. Set the five thresholds and it's live.
pitch_deck.s7.bsc_body: Four perspectives become metric categories. Weights reflect your strategic balance.
pitch_deck.s7.eos_body: Quarterly rocks become time-bound metrics. On track or off track gets replaced with five states.
pitch_deck.s7.custom_body: If you can define what good looks like, Primer can measure it. The five-tier model adapts to any structure.
pitch_deck.s7.footnote: Migration guides ship in the docs folder. Your existing goals don't start over. They get a system.
pitch_deck.s8.title: Built for how your industry works.
pitch_deck.s8.construction_body: A 200-person firm runs Primer across four project sites. Safety weighted at 40%. Plant managers open it every Monday because the metrics are theirs.
pitch_deck.s8.pe_body: An operating partner deployed Primer at five portfolio companies in ten days. One license per portco. Standard scorecard, zero new SaaS contracts.
pitch_deck.s8.manufacturing_body: A German manufacturer runs Primer air-gapped in the factory. Works council approved it because the data never crosses a border.
pitch_deck.s9.title: The five-year number.
pitch_deck.s9.sub: Based on 100 employees at $15/user/month, the industry average for bundled performance tools.
pitch_deck.s9.highlight_sub: saved over five years for a 100-person company. The number gets worse for SaaS as you grow.
pitch_deck.s9.footnote: One payment of $5,000. No per-seat fees. No renewal. If it works, you keep it forever.
pitch_deck.s10.title: What ships in the box.
pitch_deck.s10.sub: Complete source code. Your infrastructure. No dependencies on DavidPM after purchase.
pitch_deck.s10.source_body: TypeScript, SvelteKit, PostgreSQL. Every line readable, auditable, modifiable.
pitch_deck.s10.deploy_body: Direct install, Docker Compose, or integrate into your existing infrastructure. Pick what fits.
pitch_deck.s10.languages_body: English, Spanish, Chinese, Arabic, French, German, Japanese, Portuguese, Korean. Full RTL support included.
pitch_deck.s10.airgap_body: No telemetry, no license server, no phone-home. Works on fully disconnected networks.
pitch_deck.s10.rebrand_body: Colors, logo, wording, language files. One config file, one rebuild, full rebrand.
pitch_deck.s10.ai_body: Non-technical buyers paste the README into any AI assistant and follow step-by-step guidance.
pitch_deck.s11.title: Up and running in weeks, not months.
pitch_deck.timeline.week1_body: License arrives as a zip file. Unzip, read the guide, done.
pitch_deck.timeline.week2_body: One Docker command on any server you already have. IT signs off.
pitch_deck.timeline.week3_body: Pick a pilot team. Leaders propose their own metrics. Managers approve.
pitch_deck.timeline.week4_body: Pilot team is running. Expand to other teams whenever you're ready.
pitch_deck.s12.title: Ready to own it?
pitch_deck.s12.body: Try the interactive demo or run the cost calculator with your real numbers.
hierarchy.bulk.guidance_body: This importer creates a basic starting point for your organizational structure. For production use, we recommend integrating directly with your HR system (Workday, BambooHR, Active Directory, etc.) to keep your hierarchy in sync automatically. Parent references are matched by name, so list parent rows before their children.
